context: the God of this world, that's a little g, God of this world, that's Satan, hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel should shine in unto them.
point_summary: Satan's blinding of unbelievers' minds explains why Christians must approach the lost with compassion rather than superiority, recognizing their condition as victims of spiritual warfare.

context: Notice what the Bible says in 2 Corinthians chapter four. You can look at a couple of verses here in this passage of scripture.
point_summary: Relying on God's power rather than one's own strength is what enables believers to serve under pressure without fainting, as Paul models in 2 Corinthians 4.

context: being troubled on every side and being perplexed. But he says that they're not distressed and they're not in despair.
point_summary: Even amid pressure and perplexity on every side, believers do not despair because the same power that renews the inward man daily is the divine strength on which ministry depends.

context: therefore seeing we have this ministry as we have received mercy. Notice those words, we faint not.
point_summary: Receiving mercy as the basis of ministry means the believer's endurance depends not on personal strength but on divine sustaining grace, linking the Greek word for "faint not" directly to Paul's charge in 2 Thessalonians 3:13.

context: Though the outward man may perish, that the inward man is renewed day by day
point_summary: The inward man's daily renewal (2 Cor. 4:16) is invoked in closing prayer as the spiritual resource that sustains believers against weariness in well-doing.

context: for he hath made him to be sin for us. That's Jesus Christ, the sinless lamb of God, God in flesh, who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.
point_summary: At the moment of salvation, God's righteousness is imputed to the believer's account because Christ, who knew no sin, was made sin so that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.

context: To be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: For the believer, death is not loss but relocation — to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord, making the language of "losing" a saved loved one a misnomer.

reference_text: to be absent from this body is to be present with the Lord
context: ...to be absent from this body is to be present with the Lord...
point_summary: Bodily death for the believer means immediate, conscious presence with the Lord, so the saved are not lost but precisely located — with the Savior — making grief a sorrow with hope rather than without it.

context: Paul says to the believers in Corinth, he says, today is the day of salvation.
point_summary: The urgency of Paul's declaration that "today is the day of salvation" is applied as a call to settle one's eternal standing without delay, given the unpredictable brevity of life.

context: God refers to Satan in 2 Corinthians chapter number 4 as the god of this world
point_summary: Satan's title "god of this world" (2 Cor. 4) is invoked to show that he imitates rather than creates, stealing and replicating the kingdom principles God establishes for his people.

context: 2 Corinthians chapter 10 tells us about the way
point_summary: Satan's strategy of imitation is underscored by his ability to transform himself, as noted in 2 Corinthians 10, into an angel of light and a minister of Christ.

context: He tells us that we're to bring every thought into the captivity, unto the obedience of Christ.
point_summary: Bringing every thought into captivity to the obedience of Christ is presented as the essential discipline by which believers guard their minds against worldly affections and avoid the pitfalls of worldliness.

reference_text: while we look not at these things which are temporal
context: the one that we find in 2 Corinthians chapter number 4 while Paul says, while we look not at these things which are temporal...
point_summary: Those who do the will of God maintain an eternal focus—looking not at temporal things but at what is eternal—and therefore do not fit into the passing world system.

context: The Bible says this in 2nd Corinthians 9 and verse number 15, thanks be unto God for his unspeakable gift.
point_summary: God's gift of eternal life surpasses all human description, as Paul's word "unspeakable" confirms that no language fully captures what is contained within it.

context: 2nd Corinthians 9 for his unspeakable gift.
point_summary: God's gift of eternal life surpasses all human description, as Paul's phrase "unspeakable gift" confirms, encompassing abundant life, spiritual blessings, divine guidance, and everlasting dwelling with God.

context: but thanks be to God for his unspeakable gift
point_summary: God's gift of eternal life is called "unspeakable" precisely because it was purchased not with silver or gold but with the precious blood of Christ, placing it beyond any human price or description.

context: 2 Corinthians 9 and verse 15 said, but thanks be to God for his unspeakable gift.
point_summary: God's "unspeakable gift" of salvation, which cannot be earned or kept by human effort, calls forth praise as the believer's fitting and primary response of gratitude.

context: the Bible says that the love of Christ constraineth us, because we thus judge that if one died for all, then we're all dead.
point_summary: Christ's love constraining the believer to live not for self but for him who died and rose is presented as the fruit of salvation received, not the means of obtaining or retaining it.

context: And notice verse number 15, in that he died for all. Remember, we said the gift is available. It's available to all. He died for all, that they which live, I've been saved, I've been born again, it says that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which died for them and rose again.
point_summary: Those who have received the gift of salvation are compelled not to live for themselves but for Christ who died and rose for them — service as the result of salvation, not its condition.

context: We find that 1 Corinthians and 2 Corinthians both deal with this teaching and this truth with regard to the judgment seat of Christ
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: God hath made him Jesus to be sin for us who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: Christ's righteousness is imputed to believers because God made the sinless Son to be sin in our place, satisfying sin's penalty and enabling reconciliation with God.

reference_text: for he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God through him
context: Listen, we speak about the imputed righteousness for he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God through him and in him.
point_summary: Christ's one sacrifice accomplishes positional sanctification, imputing His righteousness to believers so that they stand before God as righteous through Him.

context: to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: Dying in faith as a stranger and pilgrim on earth leads to one of two certain destinations: the rapture or, through death, immediate presence with the Lord.

context: The Bible says in second Corinthians five in verse number 20, now then we are. ambassadors for Christ.
point_summary: Believers are Christ's ambassadors on earth, holding dual citizenship but charged with the ministry of reconciliation—calling others to be reconciled to God through Christ.

context: says at the end of that verse, as though God did beseech you by us, we pray you in Christ's stead be reconciled to God.
point_summary: Believers are Christ's ambassadors entrusted with the ministry of reconciliation, making gospel proclamation—not political engagement—the primary purpose of their earthly citizenship.

context: And it reminds us just a couple of verses earlier that God said this, that all things are of God who hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ and hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation.
point_summary: God has given reconciled believers the ministry of reconciliation, making the proclamation of the gospel—not political engagement—the primary purpose of the church's presence on earth.

context: God made him sin who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: Christ's substitutionary exchange—bearing sin so believers receive God's righteousness—is cited as the doctrinal ground establishing that salvation must precede baptism.

context: Remember, the Antichrist, or that spirit of Antichrist, we know that there will be an Antichrist, but the spirit of Antichrist is already working, and we find that the attack of the enemy, the desire of the enemy, is to blind people from truth, to keep them in darkness. That's 2 Corinthians chapter number four, and there are many that would follow their pernicious ways.
point_summary: The enemy's strategy against false teaching is identified as blinding people to truth and keeping them in darkness, drawn from 2 Corinthians 4.

context: comparing themselves among themselves
point_summary: Self-examination at the Lord's Table must be measured against Christ and his Word as the standard, not by comparing ourselves among ourselves, which Paul calls unwise.

context: we are troubled on every side, yet not distressed. We are perplexed, but not in despair.
point_summary: Paul's testimony in affliction — troubled yet not distressed, perplexed yet not in despair — illustrates that the classroom of affliction does not destroy but rather manifests the life of Christ in the believer.

context: always bearing about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body.
point_summary: Bearing about in the body the dying of Jesus—Paul's catalogue of sufferings—frames the affliction he later calls "light," establishing the paradox that severe suffering can be reframed through the lens of Christ's own death and resurrection life.

context: for which cause we faint not, but though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed day by day.
point_summary: Even amid catalogued sufferings, the inward man's daily renewal by God enables Paul to characterize his afflictions as light and momentary rather than crushing or permanent.

context: for our light affliction, which is but for a moment.
point_summary: Paul's description of his sufferings as a "light affliction" and "but for a moment" is held up as a striking perspective that challenges how believers typically experience and characterize their own trials.

context: in 2 Corinthians chapter number 11, really beginning in verse number 21 and going down through verse number 28
point_summary: Paul's catalog of sufferings in 2 Corinthians 11:21–28 is cited as the backdrop that makes his description of those same sufferings as a "light affliction" all the more striking in its eternal perspective.

context: in 2 Corinthians chapter number 11, really beginning in verse number 21 and going down through verse number 28
point_summary: Paul's extensive catalog of sufferings in 2 Corinthians 11:21–28 is cited as the backdrop that makes his description of affliction as "light" and "but for a moment" all the more striking in its eternal perspective.

context: number 17 here in 2 Corinthians 4, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory.
point_summary: Present affliction, though momentarily painful, is reframed by Paul as productive—working toward a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory that outweighs the suffering itself.

context: we're familiar with the passage of Scripture, and often we quote part of verse number 9 when it says, my grace is sufficient for thee.
point_summary: God's grace declared sufficient in 2 Corinthians 12:9 is shown here to be only part of the passage, as the sermon presses beyond the familiar phrase to examine the thorn itself and Paul's repeated, unanswered plea for its removal.

context: In verse number 7, the Bible says that Paul is sharing his testimony and says, lest I should be exalted above measure through the abundance of the revelations.
point_summary: God's purpose in Paul's thorn was not punishment but prevention of pride, given precisely because the abundance of revelations made humility necessary.

context: He, God, said unto me, my grace is sufficient for thee, for my strength is made perfect in weakness.
point_summary: God's answer to Paul's repeated prayer for relief was not removal of the thorn but the assurance that divine grace is sufficient and strength is perfected precisely in weakness.

context: most gladly therefore will I rather glory in my infirmities that the power of Christ may rest upon me. Therefore, I take pleasure in infirmities, in reproaches, in necessities, in persecutions, in distresses for Christ's sake. For when I am weak, then am I strong.
point_summary: Paul's affliction became a classroom where he learned that divine grace is sufficient and that God's strength reaches its perfection precisely in human weakness.

context: therefore I take pleasure in infirmities, in reproaches, in necessities, in persecution, in distresses for Christ's sake. Why? Because when I am weak, then am I strong.
point_summary: Affliction taught Paul that weakness is the very condition through which Christ's power rests upon the believer, making boasting in infirmities a reasoned, learned response rather than mere resignation.

reference_text: third chapter of the book of 2 Corinthians
context: In this third chapter of the book of 2 Corinthians, we find, really, that throughout the chapter, there's this contrast that is being given to us, and it's a contrast between law and grace.
point_summary: Second Corinthians 3 presents a sustained contrast between law and grace, tracing from Moses and Exodus to the believer's present standing under grace as declared in Romans 6:14.

context: as is spoken of here in 2 Corinthians chapter number 3, was glorious.
point_summary: Second Corinthians 3 presents a sustained contrast between law and grace, showing that the law, though genuinely glorious, was limited and could not give life, whereas believers now live under grace.

context: ink, but with the spirit of the living God, not in tables of stone, but in fleshly tables of the heart.
point_summary: Believers themselves, written by the Spirit of the living God on fleshly hearts rather than stone, serve as the living credential validating Paul's ministry over any written letter of commendation.

context: not that we are sufficient of ourselves to think anything as of ourselves, but our sufficiency is of God
point_summary: Human sufficiency for ministry is explicitly denied — all adequacy comes from God alone, answering the question raised in the previous chapter about who is sufficient for these things.

context: who also hath made us able ministers of the New Testament, not of the letter, but of the spirit. For the letter killeth, but the spirit giveth life.
point_summary: The letter of the law condemns and kills, while the Spirit gives life — establishing the sermon's first main point that the law condemns rather than delivers.

context: the letter killeth, but the spirit giveth life
point_summary: The letter of the law kills by condemning sinners, while the Spirit gives life — establishing that law and grace cannot be mingled as the only means of salvation.

context: for if the ministration of condemnation be glory
point_summary: Paul's label for the Mosaic law as "the ministration of condemnation" establishes that the law's function is to condemn, not to save, contrasting with the life-giving work of the Spirit.

context: the letter killeth
point_summary: The law, described as "the letter" that kills, functions as a ministration of condemnation—exposing sin through commandments no one can fully keep, as illustrated by a personal evangelism encounter.

context: We find that in verse number seven, the Bible says of the ministration of death. Notice how he refers to it there. The ministration of death written and engraven in stones was glorious so that the children of Israel could not steadfastly behold the face of Moses for the glory of his countenance.
point_summary: The Mosaic law's glory, though real, was always temporary — designed to fade away — marking it as a provisional, shadowing covenant rather than the permanent, final word of God.

context: We see in verse number 11, the Bible says, for if that which is done away was glorious, much more that which remaineth is glorious.
point_summary: The law's glory was real but temporary — verse 11 contrasts what is "done away" with what "remaineth," establishing that the gospel dispensation surpasses the Mosaic in permanent, superior glory.

context: We find in verse number 13, and not as Moses, which put a veil over his face, that the children of Israel could not steadfastly look to the end of that, which is abolished.
point_summary: Moses veiling his face illustrates that the law's glory was temporary and destined to be abolished, not a permanent covenant but a fading shadow pointing beyond itself.

context: And so we see these three verses of scripture in verse seven, in verse 11, in verse 13, that make reference to the fact that the law, yes, it condemns, but it was also temporary.
point_summary: The fading glory of Moses' face, described in verse 7 as glory "to be done away," demonstrates that the Mosaic law was never intended to be permanent but temporary, pointing beyond itself.

context: In fact, as we come to 2 Corinthians chapter four as we deal with it next week, the Bible talks about how the God of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not.
point_summary: Introduced as next week's text, 2 Corinthians 4 is previewed as teaching that Satan blinds the minds of unbelievers—illustrated here by Israel's ongoing spiritual blindness through rejection of Messiah.

context: 'Nevertheless, when it,' 'the heart shall turn to the Lord,' 'the veil shall be taken away.'
point_summary: When a Jewish person turns to the Lord in faith, the veil of spiritual blindness is removed—just as Jewish believers today testify, salvation comes only through personal trust in Christ, not ancestry.

context: as you look at 2 Corinthians 3, it seems as if Paul, again, the idea and the teaching of progressive revelation, that Paul is helping us understand something about Exodus 34
point_summary: Paul's reading of Exodus 34 in 2 Corinthians 3 reveals that Moses veiled his face to conceal a fading glory, signifying that the Mosaic covenant's glory was always destined to be done away.

context: Paul says very plainly in 2 Corinthians 3, it condemns. It's temporary, has a fading glory.
point_summary: The Old Testament sacrificial system, with its repeated offerings and annual remembrance of sin, pointed forward to Christ but could not remove sin — a contrast Paul uses in 2 Corinthians 3 to show that the law condemns while the gospel converts.

context: in just a couple of chapters here in the book of 2 Corinthians, that Paul will write that great verse of scripture
point_summary: Contrasted with the law's "ministration of condemnation," the gospel's "ministration of righteousness" exceeds in glory because Christ became sin so believers might receive God's righteousness in him.

context: for the ministration of condemnation be glory, much more doth the ministration of righteousness, notice, exceed in glory.
point_summary: If the law's ministry of condemnation carried glory, the gospel's ministry of righteousness exceeds it immeasurably, because it imputes to sinners the very righteousness of Christ (2 Cor. 5:21).

context: for even that which was made glorious had no glory in this respect by reason of the glory that exceleth.
point_summary: The glory of the old covenant law, though real, is so eclipsed by the surpassing glory of the gospel's righteousness that it appears to have no glory by comparison.

context: He said in the beginning verses of 2 Corinthians chapter number three, do we begin again to commend ourselves,
point_summary: The transformed lives of the Corinthian believers served as Paul's living letter of commendation, making written credentials unnecessary because the gospel's converting power was visibly evident in them.

context: or need we, as some others, epistles of commendation to you or letters of commendation from you?
point_summary: The transformed lives of the Corinthian believers serve as living epistles — visible proof of the gospel's converting power — requiring no written letters of commendation.

context: Ye are our epistle, written in our hearts, known and read of all men,
point_summary: The transformed lives of the Corinthian believers serve as living epistles — visible, readable proof of the gospel's converting power — requiring no written letters of commendation.

context: for as much as ye are manifestly declared to be the epistle of Christ, ministered by us, written not with ink, but with the spirit of the living God, not in tables of stone, but in fleshly tables of the heart.
point_summary: The transformed lives of the Corinthian believers serve as living epistles — visible proof of the gospel's converting power — written not with ink but by the Spirit of the living God.

context: now the Lord is that spirit, and where the spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty
point_summary: Where the Spirit of the Lord is present among the converted, there is liberty — specifically the liberty of access to God that was once restricted to Moses alone.

context: but we all, with open face, beholding as in a glass
point_summary: Whereas only Moses could behold God's glory on the mountain, all the converted now behold that glory with unveiled faces and are progressively transformed into the same image.

context: according to 2 Corinthians 3 and verse 18, grace makes us like Christ.
point_summary: Whereas the law brings sinners to Christ by condemnation, 2 Corinthians 3:18 teaches that grace makes believers like Christ through sanctification—a progressive transformation from glory to glory by time spent in God's presence.

context: He besought the Lord thrice, and God said, Paul, my grace is sufficient for thee.
point_summary: God's grace declared sufficient to Paul in his unanswered plea illustrates that divine development through trial can be the greater purpose behind what feels like undelivered prayer.

context: If our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost
point_summary: The necessity of some contact with the lost world is grounded in the gospel's need to reach them, citing 2 Corinthians 4 to establish that the light must not be hidden from the perishing.

context: We're not ignorant of his devices is what Paul says to the church in Corinth in 2 Corinthians.
point_summary: Paul's warning that believers are "not ignorant of his devices" is cited to reinforce that the enemy's tactics are knowable, grounding the call to spiritual distancing in apostolic awareness of spiritual danger.

context: be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers for what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness
point_summary: Paul's second letter to the Corinthians extends his first letter's warning against corrupting associations into a direct command for separation, grounding the believer's distinctness in the incompatibility of righteousness with unrighteousness and light with darkness.

context: As God hath said, I will dwell in them and walk in them and I will be their God and they shall be my people.
point_summary: God's identity as the dwelling place of His people grounds the command to separate from unbelievers, since the temple of the living God has no agreement with idols.

context: Wherefore come out from among them and be ye separate saith the Lord and touch not the unclean thing and I will receive you
point_summary: God's command in 2 Corinthians 6:17 to "come out and be separate" grounds the sermon's call to "spiritual distancing"—a deliberate refusal to be influenced or tainted by the world in one's walk with God.

context: and will be a father unto you and you shall be my sons and daughters saith the Lord Almighty.
point_summary: God's promise to receive, father, and claim believers as sons and daughters is presented as the motivating reward that makes the call to separation worth heeding.

context: having therefore these promises dearly beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit perfecting holiness in the fear of God.
point_summary: The promises of divine fellowship and adoption in 2 Corinthians 6–7 ground the call to cleanse oneself from all filthiness of flesh and spirit, making separation from the world the pathway to perfecting holiness.

context: Come out from among them and be ye separate, saith the Lord.
point_summary: Separation from worldly and ungodly influences is the believer's active response to the warning against evil companionship, grounded in God's own command to "come out from among them and be ye separate."

context: What concord, what communion hath Christ with Belial?
point_summary: The rhetorical question "what concord hath Christ with Belial?" is used to underscore the absolute incompatibility between believers and ungodly associations, grounding the call to spiritual separation in stark contrast between light and darkness.

context: If any man be in Christ, he's a new creature. All things are passed away. Behold, all things are become new
point_summary: Regeneration through Christ alone produces a genuinely new creature, ruling out the notion that sincerity or any other path suffices for salvation.

context: I quoted already from 2 Corinthians 5 and verse 17
point_summary: Societal problems find their only true resolution not through political or economic means but through the gospel's converting power, which brings transformation rather than mere reformation.

context: Wherefore we labor, that whether present or absent we may be accepted of him.
point_summary: Paul's assurance of heaven in the preceding verses (vv. 1–8) becomes the motivating foundation for the labor of pleasing Christ described in verse 9, linking certainty of glory to present Christian striving.

context: We find that next week as we move forward into the text of Scripture, beginning in verse number 10, what does Paul begin to talk about? He talks about the judgment seat of Christ, that we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ
point_summary: Sandwiched between the certainty of heaven and the certainty of the judgment seat of Christ, Paul's labor to please God in 2 Corinthians 5:9 gains its urgency and context.

context: Paul said there in 2 Corinthians 5 and verse number nine that I might be accepted, that we may be accepted of him. Well-pleasing in his sight.
point_summary: Paul's ambition to be "accepted of him" in 2 Corinthians 5:9 is linked to Romans 12:1–2's call to present oneself as a living sacrifice, with "acceptable unto God" defining the standard of a life pleasing to him.

context: And just a few verses in 2 Corinthians chapter 5 and verse number 15, a verse that's really my favorite Bible verse, and that He died for all that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto Him which died for them and rose again.
point_summary: Christ's death obligates believers to abandon self-directed living and surrender fully to Him — illustrated by Paul's usefulness to God flowing directly from that surrender.

context: we labor, that whether present or absent, we may be accepted of Him
point_summary: Paul's use of "we labor" in verse 9 reveals that genuine desire to please Christ demands dedicated, ongoing effort—not passive intention.

context: What did we see over in 2nd Corinthians 5 and verse number 9? He says wherefore Wherefore we labor
point_summary: Pleasing God requires constant, strenuous labor—the word "labor" in 2 Corinthians 5:9 paralleling Paul's self-described "exercise" in Acts 24:16—both pointing to a conscience kept void of offense toward God and men.

context: for our rejoicing is this the testimony of our conscience that in simplicity and godly Sincerity not with fleshly wisdom, but by the grace of God We have had our conversation in the world and more abundantly to you word
point_summary: When falsely accused, Paul could defend himself by first examining his own conscience rather than immediately explaining himself, finding it clear before God.

context: we therefore we labor We labor this was something that was ongoing
point_summary: Paul's ongoing, dedicated labor to be accepted of Christ — whether present or absent — is presented as the governing ambition that makes self-examination before God a regular, necessary discipline of the Christian life.

context: my grace is sufficient for thee
point_summary: Magnifying God rather than personal achievement guards against pride, as Paul—despite extraordinary revelations—deliberately refused to glory in himself, lest he be exalted above measure.

context: of such in one will I glory, yet of myself I will not glory, but in mine infirmities
point_summary: Paul's deliberate choice to glory only in his infirmities rather than his extraordinary revelations is presented as the antidote to the pride that destroyed Uzziah.

context: for though I would desire to glory, I shall not be a fool, for I will say the truth
point_summary: Paul's deliberate refusal to boast beyond what others could observe of him illustrates how magnifying God rather than oneself guards against the pride that destroyed Uzziah.

context: my grace is sufficient for thee, for my strength is made perfect in weakness
point_summary: God's grace sustaining Paul through weakness, rather than delivering him from it, redirected all glory away from Paul so that Christ alone would be magnified.

context: I could have been exalted above measure
point_summary: God's gift of a thorn to Paul was a gracious restraint against pride, ensuring that Christ, not Paul, would be magnified through his weakness.

context: lest that any man should think of me above that which he seeth me to be, or that he heareth of me
point_summary: Paul's refusal in 2 Corinthians 12:6 to let anyone think of him beyond what they actually observe is presented as the posture of genuine humility—deflecting personal credit so that God alone is magnified.

context: to be absent from this body, means to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord
context: But we know that Paul says that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: For believers, death is not hopeless loss but confident transition, because Paul's assurance that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord transforms a funeral into a celebration.

context: and the Bible says there, and that he died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which died for them and rose again.
point_summary: Christ's death obligates believers to abandon self-directed living and live wholly for him — a truth the preacher identifies as his personal life verse and daily governing commitment.

context: As I have summarized this verse of scripture, 2 Corinthians 5, and verse number 15, I've summarized it this way so that it would fit on a polo. Living for him who died for me.
point_summary: Christ's death obligates believers to live not for themselves but for him, a truth the preacher calls his life verse and summarizes as "living for him who died for me."

reference_text: to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord
context: he was going to put off this earthly tabernacle, and he was going to go to be with Jesus, his savior, for he said, to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Crossing the finish line of life's race means departing the body to be present with the Lord, a truth Paul embraced as he neared his own death.

context: My theme verse for my life, 2 Corinthians 5 and verse number 15, and Mady has made some things for me over the years, knowing that that's my favorite Bible verse, and I've summarized it, because to write the whole verse out is just too much to put on a shirt or something.
point_summary: Living for Christ who died for us is the animating purpose behind enduring hardship in ministry, summarized as "living for him who died for me."

reference_text: we look not at those things which are temporal, but those things which are eternal
context: we look not at those things which are temporal, but those things which are eternal
point_summary: Fixing one's focus on eternal rather than temporal things is the proper posture for those who already possess eternal life, and therefore ought to live and profess accordingly.

context: His strength is made perfect in weakness. 2 Corinthians 12.
point_summary: God's strength made perfect in weakness grounds the call to persevere, reminding believers that divine power—not personal endurance—sustains the race when they want to give up.

context: Paul is referencing some visions, according to verse number one, that he, visions and revelations that are received of the Lord.
point_summary: Paul's visions and revelations in verse 1 are noted as genuine yet restricted in disclosure, contrasting with modern claims of private revelation that lack the same apostolic warrant.

context: He also mentions in the text of Scripture that he was not able, it was unlawful for him to speak or to utter these things.
point_summary: Paul's heavenly visions were so extraordinary that he was forbidden to speak of them, a restraint the sermon uses to caution against unverified claims of personal revelation.

context: the Bible also tells us in this very book that the devil, the enemy, he transforms himself into an angel of light.
point_summary: Satan's ability to disguise himself as an angel of light is cited as a warning to test claimed visions and revelations, since Paul himself was forbidden to speak of what he received.

context: and lest I should be exalted above measure through the abundance of the revelations, notice, there was given to me a thorn in the flesh.
point_summary: Paul's acknowledgment that the thorn "was given to him" establishes that his suffering was purposeful and divinely ordained, not accidental, serving as the ground for the broader claim that nothing in life happens by chance.

context: Now notice in verse eight, for this thing,
point_summary: Paul's repeated, specific prayer for the thorn's removal—offered three times—demonstrates that persistent, earnest petition for relief is a legitimate and expected response to suffering.

context: And he said unto me, My grace is sufficient for thee: for my strength is made perfect in weakness.
point_summary: God's answer to Paul's earnest, repeated prayer for relief was not removal of the thorn but a promise that divine grace and strength perfected in weakness are sufficient for every difficulty.

context: Therefore I take pleasure in infirmities, in reproaches, in necessities, in persecutions, in distresses for Christ's sake: for when I am weak, then am I strong.
point_summary: Paul's paradoxical declaration that weakness becomes the very condition of divine strength grounds the sermon's call to find sufficiency in God's grace rather than in the removal of difficulty.

context: God says to Paul, my grace is sufficient for thee.
point_summary: Unlike human grace that can be tested to exhaustion, God's grace declared to Paul — "my grace is sufficient for thee" — is limitless and can never be depleted by any individual or collective need.

context: my grace is sufficient for thee
point_summary: God's grace toward Paul—and toward believers in weakness—is not merely abundant but limitless, incapable of being exhausted, and personally sufficient for every thorn-bearing trial.

context: And He says, my strength is made perfect in weakness.
point_summary: God's omnipotent strength—the same power that spoke light into existence—is made perfect precisely in human weakness, meaning our inability becomes the condition for his sufficiency.

context: God's strength is made perfect in weakness
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: My grace is sufficient for thee. My strength is made perfect in weakness.
point_summary: The present-tense verb "is" in Christ's promise anchors God's sufficient grace not in past experience or future hope but in the believer's current moment of weakness.

reference_text: My grace is sufficient for thee
context: So you know what God says? My grace is sufficient for thee.
point_summary: The present-tense verb "is" in "My grace is sufficient for thee" establishes that God's grace meets every need not in the past or future, but in the believer's present circumstance, today.

context: my grace is sufficient for thee, for my strength is made perfect in weakness
point_summary: God's answer to Paul's threefold prayer was not a bare refusal but the promise "my grace is sufficient," a truth the sermon insists must be actively seized and embraced, not treated as a Christian cliché.

context: But notice what Paul says in 2 Corinthians chapter 1 and verse number 20, for all the promises of God in Him are yea, and in Him, amen, unto the glory of God by us.
point_summary: Every promise of God—including the sufficiency of His grace—is confirmed as "yea and amen" in Christ, meaning these promises must be personally embraced, not merely recited as clichés.

context: And he, speaking of God, said to me, speaking of Paul, my grace is sufficient for thee. For my strength is made
point_summary: God's promise in 2 Corinthians 12:9 must be personally embraced—not merely memorized or recited—so that in every situation one can gladly glory in weakness because His grace is sufficient.

context: my grace is sufficient for Thee, that my strength is made perfect in weakness
point_summary: Paul's divine revelation that God's strength is perfected in weakness moved him to gladly glory in his infirmities rather than complain, embracing weakness as the very condition through which Christ's power rests upon him.

context: he said my grace is sufficient
point_summary: God's grace meets the believer precisely at the moment of need—not in anticipation of hypothetical crises—and because it cannot be exhausted, it is sufficient for whatever is actually faced.

context: God's grace is sufficient. Let's pray. Father, we love you. How we thank you for loving us. God, I pray this morning that just as Paul embraced this promise that was explained in response to his request that this thorn in his flesh would depart from him.

context: your grace is sufficient
point_summary: God's grace declared sufficient in 2 Corinthians 12:9 is presented as a promise to be personally embraced, not merely intellectually understood, by believers facing difficulties and hardships.

context: ...though I would desire to glory...

context: when God says, my grace is sufficient for thee

context: Verse number 5, of such in one will I glory. I'm not going to glory myself.

context: Notice the last part of verse 6, it's so important, lest any man should think of me above that which he seeth me to be, or that he heareth of me.

context: We find this evening that Paul takes this thing of humility to a different level in 2 Corinthians chapter 12, a level by which revealing to me the means by which God was able to use him in such a powerful way.
point_summary: Paul's humility extended beyond refusing self-glory to actively resisting others' inflated praise of him, lest anyone think of him above what they actually saw or heard.

point_summary: God made Christ to be sin for us so that believers might be made the righteousness of God in Him — the good news that conquers the fear of death.

context: the ministry that he spoke of in 2 Corinthians chapter 4 in verse number 1
point_summary: The ministry described in 2 Corinthians 5:18–20 is presented as the fuller development of the same ministry Paul referenced in 2 Corinthians 4:1, where he declares that those who have received mercy do not faint.

context: now then we are ambassadors for Christ as though God did
point_summary: Believers are called Christ's ambassadors entrusted with a reconciling appeal—God Himself beseeching sinners through them—making gospel proclamation both a privilege and a weighty responsibility.

context: the Bible says in verse 18, we spoke already in the past two weeks about verses 14 and 15 and that he died for all in verse 15 that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves but unto him which died for them and rose again. point_summary: The ministry of reconciliation in verse 18 is entrusted exclusively to the reconciled—not angels, but those who have themselves been reconciled to God through Christ.

context: the Bible says in verse 18, we spoke already in the past two weeks about verses 14 and 15 and that he died for all in verse 15 that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves but unto him which died for them and rose again.

point_summary: Reconciliation with God through Christ—being made a new creature—is the prerequisite that qualifies believers, and no one else, to engage in the ministry of reconciliation.

context: Notice with me in verse 18, the Bible says at the end of the verse, and hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation.
point_summary: Reconciled believers have been entrusted with a specific purpose—the ministry of reconciliation—which defines why the ambassador title carries both privilege and responsibility.

context: Verse 19, to wit that God was in Christ
point_summary: The ambassador's purpose is grounded in God's own reconciling act in Christ, which then issues in the specific commission—the word of reconciliation—entrusted to believers.

point_summary: God's act of reconciling the world to himself in Christ defines the ambassador's purpose: carrying the committed "word of reconciliation" to those not yet reconciled.

context: Now then, verse 20, we are ambassadors for Christ. As though God did beseech you by us, we pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God.
point_summary: The ambassador's purpose is reconciliation — a work the believer cannot accomplish personally but carries as a God-given ministry, pleading on Christ's behalf for sinners to be reconciled to God.

context: we pray you in Christ's stead be ye reconciled to God
point_summary: Bearing the title of ambassador carries the urgent, personal responsibility of pleading with individuals—as Christ's own representative—to be reconciled to God, a duty that extends beyond distributing tracts to active, heartfelt entreaty.

context: we pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God
point_summary: The ambassador's calling in verse 20 demands personal, urgent pleading—not merely distributing literature—because "pray you in Christ's stead" implies beseeching individuals, not functioning as a marketing agent.

context: Second Corinthians 10 and verse number 3, for though we walk in the flesh, we do not war after the flesh.
point_summary: Spiritual warfare in interpersonal conflict requires bringing every thought into captivity to Christ, not merely addressing the human party involved, because the real battle is not fought in the flesh.

context: The Bible tells us as Paul is writing to the believers in 2 Corinthians, he says we're not ignorant of his devices.
point_summary: Believers are not ignorant of Satan's devices—his chief strategy being to convince God's people that one another, rather than he himself, is the true enemy.

context: again in the book of 2nd Corinthians he says examine yourselves
point_summary: Paul's call to "examine yourselves" in 2 Corinthians is presented as a neglected but essential discipline of the Christian life, linked to the Lord's Supper and the pursuit of holiness.

context: In 2 Corinthians 10 and verse number five, he says that we're to bring into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ.
point_summary: Bringing every thought into captivity to Christ's obedience (2 Cor. 10:5) is presented as the governing standard by which believers must evaluate what they read, watch, and listen to.

context: Be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers. For what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness? What communion hath light with darkness?
point_summary: Paul's rhetorical questions in 2 Corinthians 6:14 are read as establishing that no legitimate union or association exists between believers and unbelievers, grounding a call to separation.

context: What concord hath Christ with Belial? Or what part hath he that believeth with an infidel?
point_summary: The rhetorical questions of 2 Corinthians 6:14–15 are read as establishing that no legitimate union or association exists between the believer and unbelief, grounding a call to separation.

context: What agreement hath the temple of God with idols? For ye are the temple of the living God. As God hath said, I will dwell in them and walk in them and I will be their God and they shall be my people.
point_summary: God's people, as the living temple of God, have no fitting agreement with idols, and this incompatibility grounds the call to separate from ungodly associations.

context: Come out from among them and be ye separate, saith the Lord. He then gives these wonderful promises. He says this, and touch not the unclean thing and I will receive you
point_summary: Separation from ungodly associations is the condition God attaches to the promises of fellowship and fatherly relationship in verses 17–18, not the condition of salvation itself.

context: and will be a father unto you and you shall be my sons and daughters, saith the Lord Almighty.
point_summary: God's promise to be Father and to receive believers as sons and daughters in verse 18 is conditioned, in this sermon's reading, on the separation from ungodly associations called for in the preceding verses.

reference_text: be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers
context: and we're not to be, be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers, and he goes through the list.
point_summary: Separation from sin does not sever the believer's relationship with God but does hinder fellowship with Him, making the command against unequal yoking a call to guard that communion.

context: I think about what we read in 2 Corinthians 6, we read the latter part of the chapter, but in chapter number 7 and verse number 1, the Bible says this, Paul's instruction to
point_summary: Cleansing from all filthiness of flesh and spirit (2 Cor. 7:1) demands active, decisive disposal of what defiles—not merely identifying the problem but resolving it completely.

context: for He hath made Him, that's God the Father, made Him, God the Son, to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in Him
point_summary: Christ's sinlessness and our sinfulness are exchanged in substitutionary atonement: God made the sinless Son to be sin so that sinners might receive the righteousness of God.

context: to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Paul's confidence that death means immediate presence with the Lord grounds the sermon's assurance that the believer's resurrection hope is certain, not merely theoretical.

context: If any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. Old things are passed away. Behold, all things are become new.
point_summary: Paul's conversion on the Damascus road is presented as the living fulfillment of 2 Corinthians 5:17—his immediate surrender of his own agenda for God's demonstrated that old things had genuinely passed away.

context: my grace is sufficient for thee
point_summary: God's grace extends beyond salvation into daily sufficiency for service, as Paul's experience of God's sustaining grace in weakness illustrates that grace empowers believers to fulfill what God calls them to do.

context: 2 Corinthians, chapter number 5, there's a great discourse here in this passage of Scripture with regard to the certainty that Paul had of eternal life
point_summary: Eternal life's certainty is not presumption but the believer's assured hope, grounded in Paul's own confidence that physical death is merely a threshold into the presence of God.

context: We find here in 2 Corinthians chapter number 5, notice how it begins in verse number 1, for we know.
point_summary: Paul's use of "we know" — not "we think" or "we hope" — grounds the believer's assurance of eternal life in the certain authority of God's Word, not in personal merit.

context: For we know that if our earthly house of this tabernacle were dissolved, we have a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens.
point_summary: Paul's use of "we know" — not "we think" or "we hope" — grounds the believer's confidence in heaven not in personal merit but in the authoritative Word of God.

context: For in this we groan, earnestly desiring to be clothed upon with our house which is from heaven.
point_summary: Paul's groaning in verse 2 is read as the believer's deep, Spirit-born longing for heaven, where the presence of Jesus himself is what makes heaven heaven.

context: if so be that being clothed, we shall not be found naked

context: For we that are in this tabernacle do groan, being burdened, not for that we would be unclothed, but clothed upon that notice when he says that mortality might be swallowed up of life
point_summary: The groaning of believers in mortal bodies (v. 4) points toward the certainty of eternal life, illustrated by D.L. Moody's declaration that death is mortality being swallowed up by life.

context: he that hath wrought us for the self, same thing as God, who also hath given unto us the earnest of the Spirit
point_summary: God's gift of the indwelling Holy Spirit at salvation serves as the earnest—the down payment and guarantee—of the believer's eternal inheritance, confirming that mortality will be swallowed up by life.

context: We see it here in 2 Corinthians 5.
point_summary: Mortality being "swallowed up of life" grounds the confidence that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord, with the indwelling Holy Spirit as the earnest guaranteeing that inheritance.

context: for we walk by faith, not by sight
point_summary: Walking by faith rather than by sight grounds Paul's confidence that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord—a certainty rooted in the indwelling Holy Spirit as earnest of the believer's inheritance.

context: we are confident, I say, and willing, rather to be absent from the body and to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Paul's confidence in the face of death rested on knowing that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord—a certainty grounded in grace received, not merit earned.

context: Paul, when he mentions about he has this eternal perspective in Second Corinthians, chapter number four, he's not looking at just the temporary, he's looking at the eternal.
point_summary: Paul's eternal perspective in 2 Corinthians 4—looking beyond the temporary to the eternal—is invoked to frame Onesimus's departure as a passing season that cannot control what God intends forever.

context: for the love of Christ constraineth us, because we thus judge, that if one died for all, then we're all dead.
point_summary: The love of Christ is identified as the very force that compels or constrains the believer to live a surrendered life, grounded in the reality that all were spiritually dead before Christ died for them.

context: The love of Christ constraineth us because we thus judge that if one died for all, then we're all dead. That word constraineth, we talked about the definition of surrender, to give or deliver up possession upon compulsion. or demand, and really, it's that being compelled or being constrained, and the word here in 2 Corinthians 5 and verse number 14, to the love of Christ constraineth us, it really carries two ideas with it.
point_summary: Christ's love "constraining" believers carries a dual force: it urges with irresistible power toward surrender while simultaneously holding the surrendered life together under its control.

context: and that he died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which died for them, and rose again.
point_summary: Christ's death for all, paired with his resurrection, is the constraining force that compels believers to abandon self-directed living and surrender wholly to him.

context: Speaking of the churches of Macedonia in this particular passage of scripture, as Paul is making an appeal to the church at Corinth.
point_summary: The Macedonian churches serve as a New Testament example of surrendered lives, with their giving cited as evidence that grace had been bestowed on them through genuine yielding to God.

context: moreover, brethren, we do you to wit of the grace of God bestowed on the churches of Macedonia
point_summary: The Macedonian churches serve as a New Testament example of surrendered living, demonstrating that genuine yield to God produces abundant joy even amid great affliction and deep poverty.

context: how that in a great trial of affliction, the abundance of their joy and their deep poverty abounded under the riches of their liberality
point_summary: Extreme affliction and deep poverty in the Macedonian churches paradoxically produced abundant joy, illustrating that genuine surrender to God yields spiritual fruit that defies natural expectation.

context: praying us with much entreaty that we would receive the gift and take upon us the fellowship of the ministering to the saints

context: and this they did not as we hoped. But notice this, and I have it circled in my Bible, but first gave their own selves to the Lord and unto us by the will of God

context: insomuch that we desired Titus, and as he had begun, so he would also finish in you the same grace also
point_summary: Surrendering oneself entirely to God—illustrated by the Macedonian believers who first gave their own selves to the Lord—is presented as the foundational act that makes radical generosity possible.

context: And this they did, not as we hoped, but first gave their own selves to the Lord, and unto us by the will of God.

context: Second Corinthians, chapter number one, God's grace is sufficient... notice in verse number one of Second Corinthians, chapter one.

context: for as the sufferings of Christ abound in us, so our consolation also aboundeth by Christ.

context: he's gonna go and he's gonna deal in chapter number 11, and he's gonna talk about all of these various things that he suffered

context: for as the sufferings of Christ abound in us, notice, so our consolation also aboundeth by Christ

context: we had the sentence of death in ourselves that we should not trust in ourselves, but in God, which raiseth the dead

context: who delivered us from so great a death

context: his grace is sufficient for me

context: Of the Jews, five times received I forty stripes, save one.

context: Thrice was I beaten with rods, once was I stoned. Thrice I suffered shipwreck. A night and a day I have been in the deep.

context: In journeyings often, in perils of waters, in perils of robbers, in perils by my own countrymen, in perils by the heathen, in perils in the city, in perils in the wilderness, in perils in the sea, in perils among the brethren.

context: In weariness and painfulness, in watchings often, in hunger and thirst,
point_summary: Paul's catalog of physical sufferings in 2 Corinthians 11 is cited to establish the weight of apostolic ministry before highlighting the even greater daily burden of concern for the churches.

context: And verse 15 is a verse of Scripture that for many years now I have claimed, if you will, as a life verse, if you've received any correspondence from me over the years, I repeatedly sign with this verse of Scripture, 2 Corinthians 5 and verse number 15. context: therefore, if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature

context: therefore, seeing we have this ministry, notice as we have received mercy, we faint not.

context: We are troubled on every side, yet not distressed. We are perplexed, but not in despair,

context: always bearing about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body.

context: For we which live are always delivered unto death for Jesus' sake, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our mortal flesh.

context: We having the same spirit of faith, according as it is written, I believed and therefore have I spoken. We also believe and therefore speak,

context: For all things are for your sakes, that the abundant grace might, through the thanksgiving of many, redound to the glory of God.

context: ...for which cause we faint not, but though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed day by day.

context: while we look not at the things which are seen, but the things which are not seen. For the things which are seen are temporal, but the things which are not seen are eternal.

context: the one who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God in him

context: Here, the Bible tells us in 2 Corinthians 5 in verse 21, for he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin. Notice the last part of this verse, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.

context: 'Examine yourselves,' Paul says to the Corinthian church, 'Examine yourselves,' and he had a reason for saying this, 'Examine yourselves whether you be in the faith. Prove your own selves.'

context: For we preach not ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord, and ourselves your servants for Jesus' sake.

context: We are troubled on every side, yet not distressed. We are perplexed, but not in despair.

context: Persecuted, but not forsaken. Cast down, but not destroyed.

context: Always bearing about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body.

context: For we which live are always delivered unto death for Jesus' sake, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our mortal flesh.

context: We having the same spirit of faith, according as it is written, I believed and therefore have I spoken, we also believe and therefore speak.

context: Now notice the rest of verse number 16, But though our outward man perish, and here's the key, yet the inward man is renewed day by day.

point_summary: Weakness embraced for Christ's sake becomes the very condition in which God's strength operates, illustrating why Paul could take pleasure in trials rather than faint under them.

context: Paul says again for a second time in this short passage that we faint not, he says the outward man may perish, but the inward man is renewed day by day.
point_summary: Daily renewal of the inward man, not the perishing outward man, is identified as the spiritual key that enables faithfulness through trials.

context: our light affliction, which is but for a moment worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory
point_summary: Present trials are reframed as light and momentary when set against an eternal weight of glory, calling believers to fix their focus on unseen, eternal realities rather than temporal circumstances.

context: while we look not at the things which are seen, but of the things which are not seen. For the things which are seen are temporal, but the things which are not seen are eternal.
point_summary: Fixing one's focus on unseen, eternal realities rather than temporal circumstances is presented as the perspective that sustains faithfulness through trials.

context: And we must have our inner man renewed day by day.
point_summary: Outward decay and inward renewal are contrasted to show that daily spiritual strength—not temporal circumstances—is what enables faithfulness through trials.

reference_text: to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord
context: the truth that Paul reveals to us to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Being absent from the body means being present with the Lord, cited as the believer's assured hope that the earthly journey's end brings eternal fellowship with Christ.

context: We live by faith, not by sight.
point_summary: Walking by faith rather than sight is the standard by which the sermon calls listeners to honestly evaluate whether they, like Israel in the wilderness, would pass God's test of obedience and trust.

context: and that he died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto Him which died for them and rose again.
point_summary: Christ's death obligates believers to live no longer for themselves but wholly for him who died and rose again — the verse the preacher personally signs on all correspondence as a summary of Christian devotion.

context: Paul speaks about how He loved the brethren. He said, the more I love, the less I be loved.

context: The Bible says, come out from among them and be separate, saith the Lord.

context: knowing therefore the terror of the Lord we persuade men. But we are made manifest unto God and I trust also are made manifest in your conscience.
point_summary: Knowing the terror of the Lord—the reality of eternal judgment—was the driving motive behind Paul's urgent, tearful persuasion of men toward the gospel.

context: for the love of Christ constraineth us because we thus judge that if one died for all then we're all dead
point_summary: The love of Christ as a constraining force explains why Paul—like Abraham—could not remain passive before the reality of eternal judgment, but was compelled to warn and persuade men.

context: him which died for them and rose again.
point_summary: Christ's death for all obligates believers to no longer live for themselves but to carry out the ministry of reconciliation as ambassadors entrusted with the word of reconciliation.

context: Wherefore henceforth know we no man after the flesh, yea, though we have known Christ after the flesh yet now henceforth know we him no more.
point_summary: Knowing no man after the flesh, believers are entrusted with the ministry of reconciliation — a commission grounded in Christ's atoning work that compels intercession and witness on behalf of the lost.

context: Therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. Old things are passed away, behold all things are become new.
point_summary: New creation in Christ grounds the believer's commission as an ambassador of reconciliation, entrusted with the word that God is not imputing trespasses against sinners.

context: And all things are of God who hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ and hath given to us, notice this, the ministry of reconciliation.

context: to wit that God was in Christ reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them, and hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation.
point_summary: God's act of reconciling the world to himself through Christ is the basis for the "ministry of reconciliation" entrusted to believers, paralleling Abraham's intercessory burden for those under judgment.

context: Now then we are ambassadors for Christ. As though God did beseech you by us, we pray you in Christ's stead be ye reconciled to God.

context: For he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.

context: Paul said if our gospel be hid it is hid to them that are lost.

context: but we have this treasure in earthen vessels that the excellency of the power may be of God and not of us.

reference_text: my grace is sufficient for thee
context: Remember he said to Paul my grace is sufficient for thee.
point_summary: God's grace declared sufficient for Paul becomes the pastoral anchor for the broken, with the Shunammite's refusal to leave Elisha's presence illustrating the necessity of staying in God's presence until that sufficiency is experienced personally.

context: as you begin reading through 2 Corinthians, chapter number four
point_summary: Recognizing the true nature of gospel ministry — including its source, its message, and its reconciling purpose — is what kept Paul from fainting under pressure.

context: in chapter three, he had spoken about the glorious gospel
point_summary: Paul's description in 2 Corinthians 3 of the glorious gospel—contrasting law and grace—grounds his understanding of the ministry entrusted to believers, a recognition that profoundly shaped his resolve not to faint.

context: as you come into the fifth chapter in the next couple of weeks
point_summary: Reconciliation — being brought to God through Christ and then commissioned to proclaim that same reconciliation to others — is the defining character of the ministry Paul describes in 2 Corinthians 5.

context: we preach not ourselves, but Christ Jesus, the Lord

context: we have an opportunity to be his ambassadors
point_summary: Being entrusted with the ministry of reconciliation — telling others how they too can be reconciled to God through Christ — is previewed here as the basis for the ambassador role Paul will develop in chapter five.

context: It's interesting that in the midst of all of the transparency that Paul will deal with in second Corinthians chapter number four, that that phrase is used twice.

context: we are troubled on every side, yet not distressed; we are perplexed, but not in despair;
point_summary: Paul's hardships were real but never final, because God's help meant that being "troubled on every side" stopped short of distress and being "perplexed" stopped short of despair.

context: Persecuted, but not forsaken; cast down, but not destroyed;
point_summary: Hardships are real, but they do not have the final word, because Paul's experience of being "persecuted but not forsaken" points to God's active help sustaining believers in the midst of trouble.

context: Always bearing about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body.

context: For we which live are always delivered unto death for Jesus' sake, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our mortal flesh.

context: So then death worketh in us, but life in you.
point_summary: Paul's suffering unto death for others' sake — "death worketh in us, but life in you" — is presented as the paradoxical cost and fruit of apostolic ministry.

context: For our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding
point_summary: Present afflictions are reframed as "light" and momentary precisely because they are producing an eternal weight of glory that infinitely outweighs them.

context: we find and we won't take the time to read it in 2 Corinthians 11, but in verses 24 through 28, Paul lists out a whole lot of things that he suffered
point_summary: Paul's catalog of sufferings in 2 Corinthians 11:24–28 is cited as evidence that his counsel on enduring pressure was drawn from personal experience, not secondhand knowledge.

context: He says in the 12th chapter of the book of 2 Corinthians, he says in verse number 14 and 15, behold, the third time I am ready to come to you and I will not be burdensome to you
point_summary: Paul's willingness to spend and be spent for others even while receiving less love in return illustrates the internal, relational pressures of ministry he personally endured.

context: He says in verse number eight of 2 Corinthians 4, notice he says this, we are troubled. He says this, on every side.
point_summary: Paul's declaration in verse 8 that believers are "troubled on every side" and "perplexed" is grounded in his own firsthand experience of ministry pressure, not borrowed theology.

context: And we're reminded of 2 Corinthians 12 and verse number nine that God's grace is sufficient.
point_summary: God's grace, cited from 2 Corinthians 12:9, is presented as the first and foundational form of divine provision that counterbalances the pressures believers face, keeping them troubled but not distressed.

context: while we look not at the things which are seen, but of things which are not seen
point_summary: Fixing one's gaze on unseen, eternal realities rather than visible, temporal pressures requires deliberate discipline, not passive drift.

context: for our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory

context: as we're studying 2nd Corinthians chapter number five this morning?

point_summary: Paul's life, traced through Acts and his epistles, models the posture 2 Corinthians 5 calls for: never letting reconciliation to God become commonplace, but continually rejoicing in it.

context: ...and hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation.

context: Now then we are ambassadors for Christ, as though God did beseech you by us: we pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God.

context: knowing therefore the terror of the Lord, we persuade men

context: where the Bible says, and that He died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves. But unto Him which died for them and rose again.

context: he hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation

context: hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation

context: we are ambassadors for Christ
