context: Notice that Paul says this in verse 14, now, now thanks be unto God which always causeth us to triumph in Christ and maketh manifest the savor of his knowledge by us in every place.
point_summary: God's triumph in Christ is not a conditional promise but a constant reality — Paul's "always" becoming the basis for contrasting believers who live victoriously with those experiencing repeated defeat.

context: now thanks be unto God, which always causeth us to triumph in Christ
point_summary: God, not the believer, is the source of triumph — "always causeth us to triumph in Christ" means victory belongs to Jesus, and recognizing this keeps the Christian humble.

context: now thanks be unto God. Notice this, which always causeth us to triumph, literally to have victory in Christ.
point_summary: Triumph in Christ is always available and always God-given, meaning the believer's victory is never self-generated but sourced entirely in what Christ has done.

context: Now thanks be unto God, which always causeth us to triumph in Christ, and maketh manifest the savour of his knowledge by us in every place.
point_summary: God's triumph in Christ is the source of victory Paul celebrates in verse 14, even before the fuller scene of that victory unfolds in the surrounding verses.

context: for we are unto God a sweet saver of Christ, and them that are saved and them that perish
point_summary: Believers are the fragrance of Christ to all people, but the gospel's effect divides: to those perishing it is the aroma of death, while to those being saved it is the aroma of life.

context: unto the one, we're the saver of death unto death, and to the other, the saver of life unto life
point_summary: The gospel's aroma divides humanity into two responses—life unto life for those who believe, death unto death for those who perish—because not everyone will receive Christ.

context: as he did in 2 Corinthians 2. I'm reminded of 1 John 3 in verse number eight.
point_summary: Christ's decisive defeat of sin, death, and the grave—confirmed by Colossians 2 and 1 John 3:8—establishes the source of victory, while 2 Corinthians 2 then reveals the strategy: sufficiency for experiencing that victory belongs to God alone.

context: But as we go back to 2 Corinthians chapter number two, I believe that we see the strategy for victory.
point_summary: Christ's resurrection victory over sin, death, and the grave—mirrored in Paul's triumphal procession imagery in 2 Corinthians 2—establishes that experiencing that victory requires following God's strategy, not our own sufficiency.

context: We come to this passage and we find that in verse 16, as we consider the strategy for victory, that Paul ends the verse of scripture by saying this,
point_summary: Paul's rhetorical question "who is sufficient for these things?" establishes that no believer is adequate in himself for gospel ministry, with the answer deferred to 2 Corinthians 3:5: sufficiency is of God alone.

context: not that we are sufficient of ourselves to think anything as of ourselves, but our sufficiency is of God.
point_summary: Human sufficiency for ministry finds no ground in self; Paul's answer to "who is sufficient?" is that all sufficiency belongs to God alone.

context: for we are not as many which corrupt the word of God, but as of sincerity, but as of God, in the sight of God speak we in Christ.
point_summary: Paul's strategy for victory requires handling Scripture with sincerity before God, in contrast to false teachers who corrupt the Word to manipulate and control people.

context: And now thanks be unto God, which always causeth us to triumph in Christ.
point_summary: Victory belongs to God alone, not to human effort or merit — Paul's triumph in Christ is always caused by God, making dependence on the Lord the defining mark of fruitful ministry.

context: And he says in verse 17, we are not as many which corrupt the word of God, but as of sincerity,
point_summary: Corrupting the word of God for personal gain stands in contrast to the sincerity Paul models, which requires both full dependence on God and uncompromising integrity with Scripture.

context: who is sufficient for these things? Not me, not you, our sufficiency is of God.
point_summary: Human sufficiency is explicitly denied — Paul's life of victory rested on the recognition that all adequacy for ministry comes from God, not from oneself.

context: And Paul says that he understood at the end of verse 17 that in the sight of God, speak we in Christ.
point_summary: Authentic ministry, as Paul frames it in verse 17, is accountable to God's sight rather than to human control, making manipulation incompatible with speaking "in Christ."

context: But God says that his strength is made perfect in weakness.
point_summary: God's strength being made perfect in weakness means that the very trials one would not have chosen become the occasion for divine power to be most fully displayed.

context: When I am weak, then I am strong.
point_summary: Weakness is not an obstacle to divine power but the very condition in which God's strength is made perfect, enabling believers to face whatever God allows in their path.

context: Blessed be God, even the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies and the God of all comfort, who comforteth us in all our tribulation.
point_summary: God comforts believers in their tribulation so that they in turn may comfort others, illustrating how Phoebe's role as a succourer of many reflects this divine pattern of received help flowing outward to others.

context: He, speaking of God the Father, hath made Him, speaking of God the Son, Jesus Christ, to be sin for us, who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in Him.
point_summary: Christ's bearing of humanity's sin — described as God making him "to be sin for us" so that believers might receive his righteousness — is cited as the completed atoning work that preceded and necessitated the resurrection announced at the empty tomb.

context: Well, we look not at those things which are temporal, but at those things which are eternal in verse number 18 of 2 Corinthians 4.
point_summary: Fixing one's gaze on eternal rather than temporal realities was the defining perspective that shaped Paul's entire apostolic mindset and ministry.

context: If any man be in Christ, he's a new creature. And what a joy to understand old things are passed away. Behold, all things are become new.
point_summary: Conversion's necessity follows directly from eternity's reality: union with Christ makes a person a new creature, with old things passed away and all things become new.

context: I quoted part of verse 18 a moment ago. Speaking about those things which are eternal and not those things which are temporal.
point_summary: Paul's grasp of eternity over temporal things (v. 18) is presented as the motivating conviction behind his willingness to suffer and press on in gospel proclamation.

context: It says this in verse number one. Therefore seeing we have this ministry as we have received mercy, we faint not.
point_summary: Mercy received in ministry is the ground of Paul's perseverance—because he has been shown mercy, he does not faint in the face of suffering and contention.

context: But have renounced the hidden things of dishonesty, not walking in crap. nor handling the word of God deceitfully, but by manifestation of the truth, commending ourselves to every man's conscience in the sight of God.
point_summary: Authentic gospel ministry renounces deception and handles the Word without craftiness, commending itself openly to every conscience before God — a standard Paul's Thessalonian ministry is then measured against.

context: ...but if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost.
point_summary: The hiddenness of the gospel to the lost is attributed not to the message's failure but to satanic blinding, underscoring why genuine conversion requires divine intervention.

context: And whom the God of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them.
point_summary: Satan's blinding of unbelieving minds to the gospel's light is cited as the very reason evangelistic urgency and faithful proclamation are necessary.

context: For we preach not ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord, and ourselves your servants for Jesus' sake.
point_summary: Faithful gospel proclamation centers not on the preacher but on Christ Jesus the Lord, with the minister serving as a servant for Jesus' sake rather than promoting himself.

context: For God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness, hath shined in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.
point_summary: God's creative act of commanding light out of darkness is presented as the pattern for the new-birth illumination by which sinners receive "the light of the knowledge of the glory of God," grounding the urgency of gospel proclamation.

context: But we have this treasure in earthen vessels that the excellency of the power may be of God and not of us.
point_summary: The treasure of the gospel dwelling in frail human vessels is cited to illustrate Paul's mindset—that the surpassing power in proclamation belongs to God, not to the one proclaiming.

context: Remember, we read 2 Corinthians 4, in whom the God of this world hath blinded the minds of them that believe not, right?
point_summary: Remembering that one was once spiritually blinded by the god of this world—as 2 Corinthians 4:4 describes—fuels compassion and grace toward the unsaved rather than mere debate.

context: for all the promises of God in him are yea, and in him, amen, unto the glory of God by us.
point_summary: Every promise of God finds its fulfillment in Christ—"yea" and "amen"—grounding Jacob's material increase not in circumstance but in God's covenant faithfulness to His word.

context: as we look at the truth of 2 Corinthians 1 that we are comforted by the God of all comfort so that we then can comfort others with the same comfort that we have received
point_summary: God comforts His people through adversity so that they in turn may comfort others with that same comfort—a truth the sermon applies to Jacob's experience of divine presence amid difficulty.

context: for He, speaking of God the Father, hath made Him, speaking of God the Son, to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in Him.
point_summary: God qualifies sinners for heaven not by their own merit but through the imputed righteousness of Christ, who was made sin so that believers might be made the righteousness of God in Him.

context: Be careful because the Bible tells us in 2 Corinthians that Satan turns himself into an angel of light and he's the deceiver and in Acts chapter number 10, Cornelius does receive a vision.

context: Our enemy is a deceiver. He's a fraud. He's an imposter. And his desire, according to 2 Corinthians chapter number 4, is to keep as many people blinded from the truth as possible.
point_summary: Satan's chief aim, according to 2 Corinthians 4, is to keep as many people as possible blinded from the truth.

context: I'm reminded of 2 Corinthians 11, would you take your Bibles and turn there with me
point_summary: Second Corinthians 11:13 is brought forward to show that the willful resistance to truth described in 2 Timothy 3:8 operates through deception engineered by false apostles who disguise themselves as servants of Christ.

context: I begin reading in verse number 13, 2 Corinthians 11 and verse number 13, the Bible says, for such are false apostles, deceitful workers, transforming themselves into the apostles of Christ.
point_summary: False teachers in the last days are not merely mistaken but are active deceivers who deliberately disguise themselves as legitimate apostles of Christ.

context: Notice the text continues in verse number 14, and no marvel in 2 Corinthians 11 and verse 14, for Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light.
point_summary: Satan's ability to disguise himself as an angel of light explains why false teachers can appear as ministers of righteousness, deceiving people with religious activity that falls short of true salvation.

context: Therefore, it is no great thing if his ministers also be transformed as the ministers of righteousness, whose end shall be according to their works.
point_summary: Satan's ministers disguising themselves as ministers of righteousness explains why false teachers can appear credible while leading people away from true salvation.

context: 2 Corinthians 5.21, for He, speaking of God the Father, hath made Him, speaking of Jesus Christ, to be sin for us,
point_summary: Christ's sinlessness, established by 2 Corinthians 5:21's phrase "who knew no sin," is presented as the necessary ground for His once-for-all self-offering as the sinless high priest.

context: The Bible says, therefore, if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. Old things are passed away, behold, all things are become new.

context: Paul Experienced what we're speaking of in 2nd Corinthians 5 in verse number 17. He was radically changed

context: Therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature

context: therefore, if any man
point_summary: The gospel that transformed Paul is the same gospel he expected to transform every believer, making radical life-change the normal expectation of salvation, not a special exception.

context: I am a new creature. I'm not the same.

context: Therefore, if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. All things are passed away. Behold, all things are become new.

context: If any man be in Christ, he is a new creature.

context: Paul says, therefore, if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. Old things are passed away. Behold, all things are become new.

context: We must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ,

context: for we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, that everyone may receive the things done in his body according to that he hath done, whether it be good or bad.

context: For we walk by faith, not by sight.

context: We are confident, I say, and willing, rather to be absent from the body and to be present with the Lord.

context: God has given to us, according to 2 Corinthians 5, in verse number five, the earnest of the spirit

context: God made him to be sin for us who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.

point_summary: Paul's command in 2 Corinthians 13:5 to "examine yourselves whether ye be in the faith" is brought forward as the scriptural warrant for self-examination in light of whether genuine evidences of conversion are present in one's life.

context: and that he died for all that They which live should not henceforth live unto themselves But unto him which died for them and rose again
point_summary: Christ's death obligates believers to live no longer for themselves but for him who died and rose again, a principle the sermon pairs with Paul's personal testimony in Galatians 2:20.

context: we're to walk by faith
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 2nd Corinthians chapter 5, I want to start up at verse 9 and lead up to verse 14 and even include verse 15.
point_summary: Confidence in the love of Christ is the prerequisite for being a "superb conveyor" of it, and 2 Corinthians 5:9's call to labor introduces a second constraining motivation alongside that love.

context: 2nd Corinthians chapter 5, I want to start up at verse 9 and lead up to verse 14 and even include verse 15.
point_summary: Confidence in the love of Christ is presented as the necessary foundation that positions a believer to convey that love outwardly, before the sermon pivots to the constraining power of that love in verse 14.

context: 2nd Corinthians chapter 5, I want to start up at verse 9 and lead up to verse 14 and even include verse 15.
point_summary: Confidence in the love of Christ is presented as the necessary foundation that positions a believer to convey that love outwardly, before the sermon pivots to the constraining power of that love in verses 14–15.

context: we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ that everyone may receive the things done in his body according to that he hath done whether it be good or bad
point_summary: Accountability before the judgment seat of Christ (v. 10) is presented as part of the constraining context that drives Paul's compelled, focused labor — not sentimental devotion alone.

context: knowing therefore the terror of the Lord
point_summary: The terror of the Lord and the judgment seat of Christ (vv. 10–11) form the urgent context that drives Paul's declaration that the love of Christ constrains—not merely compels—believers into focused, pressured gospel action.

context: for the love of Christ constraineth us
point_summary: Christ's love does not merely motivate but *constrains*—pressing in on every side like a narrow strait or a cattle chute, leaving no room to drift from the path of compelled obedience.

context: if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature, old things are passed away, behold, all things become new
point_summary: Regeneration in Christ (2 Cor. 5:17) grounds the refutation of antinomianism: the new creature's transformed life is the very basis on which pleasing God becomes both obligatory and genuinely attainable.

context: We're to live by faith, not by sight
point_summary: Faith is not passive sentiment but active trust in God's Word and character, expressed in daily life regardless of visible circumstances—the very substance of walking by faith, not by sight.

context: Notice the people in 2 Corinthians chapter 9, and I have to hurry here, 2 Corinthians
point_summary: Cheerful, faith-driven giving pleases God and evidences trust in Him, as seen in believers who gave generously out of deep poverty rather than abundance.

context: but this I say, he which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly, and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully
point_summary: Generous giving is presented as a matter of sowing and reaping — those who give bountifully can expect a bountiful return, just as agricultural logic demands a harvest proportionate to the seed planted.

context: every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give. But notice what it says, not grudgingly or of necessity, for God loveth a cheerful giver.
point_summary: Cheerful, purposeful giving—not reluctant or compelled—is identified as a means of pleasing God, paralleling the agricultural principle that bountifulness in sowing yields bountifulness in reaping.

context: 2 Corinthians 5.17 says this, therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. Old things are passed away, behold all things are become new.
point_summary: Salvation in Christ is not reformation but transformation — the believer becomes a new creature, with old things passed away and all things made new.

context: 2 Corinthians says, today is the day of salvation
point_summary: The urgency of 2 Corinthians 6:2 is applied as an immediate gospel invitation: today is the day a person's name can be written in the book of life through faith in Christ.

context: The Bible teaches us, and we'll look at it from our perspective with regard to save people first in 2 Corinthians chapter number five.
point_summary: Paul's certainty about eternity—grounded in divine revelation ("for we know")—is presented as the theological foundation that compelled his bold gospel proclamation in Ephesians 6:19–20.

context: he begins in verse number one, for we know.
point_summary: Paul's opening declaration "for we know" in 2 Corinthians 5:1 grounds gospel boldness in the certainty of eternity—revealed truth believers can stand on and speak with authority.

context: look at verse number four, he says, for we that are in this tabernacle do groan, being burdened, not for that we would be unclothed, but clothed upon that mortality might be swallowed up of life.
point_summary: Paul's certainty that mortality will be swallowed up of life grounds the believer's confidence in eternity and, by extension, the urgency to proclaim the gospel boldly.

context: for we are confident, I say, and willing rather to be absent from the body and to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Confident knowledge of divine revelation grounds the believer's assurance that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord, making eternity with Christ a certainty worth proclaiming.

context: But I want you to think about what Paul said. Let me look at this verse in 2 Corinthians chapter number 2, and then let me deal with the text issue.
point_summary: Paul's warning in 2 Corinthians 2:17 that many corrupt the Word of God grounds the argument that beginning with a corrupt underlying text inevitably produces a corrupt translation, regardless of the translator's scholarly qualifications.

context: But in 2 Corinthians, the Apostle Paul is writing back to the church at Corinth and he makes a very interesting statement here with regard to those that would corrupt the Word of God. And so we understand, I've already expressed the fact that Satan has attacked the Word of God from the start of human history, but Paul says this in 2 Corinthians 2 in verse 17, "'For we are not as many,' notice, "'which corrupt the Word of God, but as of sincerity, but as of God, in the sight of God, speak we in Christ.'" And so we acknowledge this evening that there are some that would corrupt the Word of God.
point_summary: Paul's warning that some "corrupt the Word of God" is applied here as biblical grounding for the argument that a corrupt underlying text inevitably produces a corrupt translation, regardless of the translator's scholarly qualifications.

context: we're not ignorant of his devices, Paul says that in 2 Corinthians
point_summary: Paul's warning that believers are "not ignorant of his devices" grounds the call to remain anchored in Scripture against the ever-varying methods of false teachers.

context: Paul says this in verse number 13, for such are false apostles.
point_summary: False apostles who promote mystical experience as the measure of salvation are, Paul warns, deceitful workers aligned with Satan's own strategy of masquerading as light.

context: But notice verse 14, in no marvel, for Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light.
point_summary: Satan's own ability to masquerade as an angel of light establishes why mysticism is gravely dangerous — its deceptions carry the authority of a supernatural counterfeit.

context: but if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost
point_summary: The hiddenness of the gospel is not a failure of the message but a mark of lostness in the hearer, making the believer's obligation to shine it outward all the more urgent.

context: we have this treasure in earthen vessels, that the excellency of the power may be of God and not of us
point_summary: The gospel itself — not human ability — is the treasure housed in frail believers, so that the surpassing power displayed in preaching belongs entirely to God.

context: if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost
point_summary: The hiddenness of the gospel to the lost is not a reason to conceal it further but the very urgency driving gospel proclamation, since Christ's resurrection gives preachers reason to let the light shine.

context: 2 Corinthians 11, beginning in verse number 16, he says, I say again, let no man think me a fool, if otherwise yet as a fool receive me that I may boast myself a little.
point_summary: Paul's "boasting" in 2 Corinthians 11:16 introduces his catalog of sufferings, cited here to show that resurrection hope produces endurance of hardship, not an "eat, drink, and be merry" worldview.

context: Are they Hebrews? So am I. Are they Israelites? So am I. Are they the seed of Abraham? So am I.
point_summary: Paul's equal standing with his opponents in Hebrew lineage and Israelite identity serves as the launching point for his catalog of sufferings, showing that his credentials exceed theirs not in pedigree but in sacrifice.

context: In labors more abundant, in stripes above measure, in prisons more frequent, in deaths oft.
point_summary: Paul's catalog of sufferings in 2 Corinthians 11:23 is read as evidence that his ministry credentials were measured not in fleshly glory but in the cost of his total devotion to Christ.

context: Of the Jews, five times received I forty stripes, save one.
point_summary: Paul's catalog of sufferings—including five scourgings of thirty-nine stripes—is presented as evidence of his unrivaled apostolic endurance and total consecration to Christ above any fleshly credential.

context: Thrice was I beaten with rods. Once was I stoned. Thrice I suffered shipwreck. A night and a day I've been in the deep.
point_summary: Paul's catalog of sufferings in 2 Corinthians 11—beatings, shipwrecks, stonings, and constant peril—is presented as evidence of his total, costly commitment to Christ rather than grounds for boasting in the flesh.

context: In journeyings often, in perils of waters, in perils of robbers, in perils by my own instrument, in perils by the heathen, in perils in the city, in perils in the wilderness,
point_summary: Paul's catalog of perils in verse 26 is presented as evidence of the extreme cost he willingly bore in faithful gospel ministry, framing the question of why anyone would endure such suffering.

context: to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Bodily death is gain for the believer because, as Paul states, to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord—reunion with the Savior who redeemed and adopted them.

context: and that He died for died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto Him which died for them and rose again.
point_summary: Christ's resurrection is the ground for self-denying, Christ-directed living — because He died and rose again, those who live should no longer live for themselves but for Him.

context: to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: Paul's confidence in Philippians 1:21 that "to die is gain" rests on the assurance expressed in 2 Corinthians 5:8 that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord.

context: for the love of Christ constraineth us because we thus judge that if one died for all, then we're all dead
point_summary: Christ's love, not mere duty, is the constraining force behind Paul's daily commitment to live for Christ rather than for himself.

context: And then he died for all that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which died for them and rose again
point_summary: Christ's death for all obligates believers to live no longer for themselves but for him — a truth the sermon presents as the personal and daily motivating commitment behind Paul's declaration in Philippians 1:21.

context: beside those things that are without, that which cometh upon me daily, the care of all the churches
point_summary: Paul's daily burden for "the care of all the churches" is cited as evidence of his consistent pastoral concern for the state of every congregation he had ministered to.

context: Look over, keep your place there in 1 Corinthians 15. We'll be back in a moment. Look over at 2 Corinthians 5. 2 Corinthians 5. I want you to, we'll pick it up in context.
point_summary: Paul's confidence that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord (2 Cor. 5:8) demonstrates how the certainty of resurrection hope shaped and sustained his life and ministry.

context: The Bible says in verse number five, now he that hath wrought us for the self, same thing as God, who also hath given unto us the earnest of the Spirit.
point_summary: The indwelling Holy Spirit given at salvation is identified as the earnest—the proof and guarantee—of the believer's redemption, grounding Paul's confident expectation of resurrection.

context: He says we walk by faith, not by sight.
point_summary: Walking by faith rather than sight is presented as the experiential reality that grounded Paul's confidence and shaped his perspective amid suffering, pointing forward to the certainty of being present with the Lord.

context: We are confident, I say, and willing, rather to be absent from the body and to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: Paul's confident willingness to depart from the body and be present with the Lord is cited as evidence that he was genuinely shaped and encouraged by the certainty of the resurrection promise.

context: we walk by faith not by sight
point_summary: Walking by faith rather than by sight, as Paul describes in 2 Corinthians 5:7, is the ongoing condition of life in the flesh that makes finishing one's course in faithfulness both a challenge and an achievement.

context: what Paul said of himself in in 2nd Corinthians 5
point_summary: Certainty of the resurrection promise (1 Corinthians 15) is meant to govern present conduct, and Paul's own example in 2 Corinthians 5 is cited as parallel evidence that eternal hope shapes active, steadfast service now.

context: But in v. 7 of chapter 5, in the book of 2 Corinthians, he says, for we walk by faith, not by sight.
point_summary: Walking by faith is a present-age calling that ends at glorification, when sight replaces faith and the believer sees the Lord directly.

context: for he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.
point_summary: Christ's sinless substitution — taking on sin he did not commit so that sinners might receive a righteousness they could not earn — is presented as the third essential truth in a gospel conversation, following conviction of sin and its penalty.

context: In 2 Corinthians chapter five, we speak about having confidence with regard to the affairs of life
point_summary: Confidence in God's sovereignty over life's events—illustrated in Joseph's backward look at evil turned to good—grounds the appeal to 2 Corinthians 5's teaching on confidence, though the passage's specific content is not yet developed in this segment.

context: Notice what Paul says with regard to his position in Christ beginning in verse number one of 2 Corinthians five. Notice these words, for we know.
point_summary: Paul's declaration "we know" grounds Christian confidence not in favorable circumstances but in the certainty that physical death yields a God-built, eternal dwelling — the same sovereign assurance Joseph displayed looking back on his suffering.

context: For we know that if our earthly house of this tabernacle were dissolved, we have a building of God, an house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens.
point_summary: Paul's certainty that death dissolves only the earthly body while God has prepared an eternal heavenly dwelling grounds the believer's confident assurance of salvation.

context: For in this we groan, earnestly desiring to be clothed upon with our house which is from heaven.
point_summary: Paul's groaning to be "clothed upon" with the heavenly dwelling illustrates the believer's confident longing for the life that swallows up mortality, grounding assurance of salvation in what God has already wrought.

context: If so be that being clothed we shall not be found naked.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: For we that are in this tabernacle do groan, being burdened: not for that we would be unclothed, but clothed upon, that mortality might be swallowed up of life.
point_summary: Mortality being "swallowed up of life" rather than death signals the believer's confident assurance of salvation, grounded in the Spirit given as earnest of the inheritance.

context: Now he that hath wrought us for the selfsame thing is God, who also hath given unto us the earnest of the Spirit.
point_summary: God's gift of the Holy Spirit at salvation serves as the earnest—the down payment—guaranteeing the believer's inheritance and confirming confident assurance of being saved.

context: therefore, notice this, we are always, do you see the word confident? Knowing that whilst we are at home in the body, we are absent from the Lord.
point_summary: Confidence in salvation, grounded in the Holy Spirit as the earnest of inheritance, means the believer walks by faith while absent from the Lord's presence in the body.

context: For we walk by faith, not by sight.
point_summary: Walking by faith rather than sight describes the believer's present condition of being in the body yet absent from the Lord, in contrast to the confident hope of being present with Him.

context: We are confident, I say, and willing, rather to be absent from the body
point_summary: Departing the body means immediate presence with the Lord — a transition Paul describes not merely as accepted but as something believers are confident in and willing to embrace.

context: to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Confidence in heaven is not mere hope but certain knowledge — to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord — and Paul's word "willing" shows the believer's eager readiness for that reality.

context: To be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: Confidence in heaven—knowing that death brings immediate presence with the Lord—is not mere hope but certain assurance, paralleling Joseph's confidence that God's sovereign plan was trustworthy.

context: Paul starts the fifth chapter of 2 Corinthians, for we know, a confidence.
point_summary: Paul's opening declaration "for we know" in 2 Corinthians 5 is cited as the biblical ground for assurance of heaven—a settled confidence, not mere hope, that believers can possess.

context: We spoke of that confidence of heaven in 2 Corinthians 5.
point_summary: Just as Joseph had confidence that God was in control, believers can have that same settled assurance—not mere hope—that heaven is their home.

reference_text: behold, today is the day of salvation.
context: Paul says in writing to the church in Corinth in 2 Corinthians, he says, behold, today is the day of salvation.
point_summary: The urgency of "today is the day of salvation" is pressed as the decisive contrast to the eternally too-late confession made at the great white throne judgment.

context: salvation to everyone, to any man, any person that would place their faith and trust in Christ. And so we find that the Bible tells us in 2nd Corinthians chapter 5 and verse number 20 that we are his ambassadors.
point_summary: Believers are called God's ambassadors—a title signifying that, like diplomatic representatives sent to a foreign nation, Christians go into this world to represent heaven, not earth.

context: in 2 Corinthians 5 and verse number 20
point_summary: Believers are called God's ambassadors because their true citizenship is in heaven, making them his representatives on earth with the mission of spreading the gospel.

context: to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: Death itself is framed as a form of divine deliverance, since to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord, meaning martyrdom does not defeat God's promise to deliver his people.

context: the outward man will perish, but it's the inward man that's renewed day by day
point_summary: Endurance through persecution is grounded not in human resolve but in the daily inward renewal described in 2 Corinthians 4:16, since the outward man perishes while spiritual strength must be continually replenished.

context: Turn to 2 Corinthians 5, and as you're turning there...
point_summary: Every believer is accountable before God for fulfilling their assigned place in His service, and no one can escape that responsibility by simply refusing to hear it.

context: And I want you to look in closing this evening at 2 Corinthians chapter 5, and I want you to understand this evening that the task that's been assigned to us is obviously quite different than the task that we read of in Judges chapter number 7.
point_summary: Divine accountability before God is universal—no one escapes it by avoiding the message—and believers carry the gospel as Christ's ambassadors, entrusted with the greatest message ever spoken.

context: Notice in verse 14, for the love of Christ constraineth us because we thus judge that if one died for all, then we're all dead.
point_summary: Christ's love, not the pursuit of heaven, is what constrains believers to live not for themselves but for Him who died and rose again.

context: live unto themselves, but unto Him which died for them and rose again.
point_summary: Because Christ died for all and rose again, those who live are called not to live for themselves but for Him — a life-altering reorientation away from the temporal and toward the eternal.

context: wherefore henceforth know we no man after the flesh, yea, though we have known Christ after the flesh, yet now henceforth know we Him no more.
point_summary: Verse 16 serves as a transitional hinge between living for self and living for Christ, pointing to a new way of knowing others no longer by fleshly standards but by eternal realities.

context: if any man be in Christ, he's a new creature, old things are passed away, behold all things are become new.
point_summary: Salvation in Christ is not self-improvement or reformation but a genuine transformation—becoming a new creature—accomplished entirely by the power of the gospel, not human effort.

context: Verse 18, and all things are of God, who hath reconciled us to Himself by Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Reconciliation with God is accomplished solely through Jesus Christ's payment for sin, and that same reconciliation is then entrusted to believers as a ministry they are commissioned to carry out.

context: therefore, seeing we have this ministry, as we have received mercy, we faint not
point_summary: Because God has entrusted believers with the ministry of reconciliation, they need not faint or lose heart, being sustained by received mercy in that calling.

context: we can be renewed in our inner man, according to verse 16 of chapter four
point_summary: Inner renewal through the Spirit sustains believers against fainting in the ministry of reconciliation, so that they need not lose heart.

context: if our gospel be hidden, it is hidden to them that are lost
point_summary: The hiddenness of the gospel to the lost is traced to Satan's active blinding of unbelieving minds, framing evangelism as spiritual warfare against a deliberate enemy of souls.

context: in whom the God of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not
point_summary: Satan's blinding of unbelieving minds is identified as the spiritual opposition that makes the ministry of reconciliation a battle, not merely a proclamation.

context: who is the image of God, should shine unto them.
point_summary: Satan's blinding of unbelieving minds (2 Cor. 4:4) is cited as evidence of the spiritual battle underlying the ministry of reconciliation, explaining why the lost remain lost without the gospel's light.

context: we preach not ourselves, but Christ Jesus, the Lord.
point_summary: The ministry of reconciliation entrusted to believers requires preaching not ourselves but Christ Jesus the Lord, since Satan blinds unbelievers specifically to keep the light of the gospel from shining to them.

context: we have this treasure in earth and vessels, that the excellency of the power may be of God, and not of us.
point_summary: God entrusts the ministry of reconciliation to frail, earthen vessels so that the surpassing power in that work belongs to God, not to the messenger.

context: we've been given this ministry of reconciliation at the end of verse 18
point_summary: God's entrustment of the ministry of reconciliation to believers means they serve as His ambassadors, representing Christ to a world whose minds Satan has blinded against the gospel.

context: God was in Christ reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them, and hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation.
point_summary: God's act of reconciling the world to himself through Christ is the basis and content of the "word of reconciliation" now committed to believers as ambassadors.

context: now then, we are ambassadors for Christ. As though God did beseech you by us, we pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God.
point_summary: Believers are called ambassadors for Christ, entrusted with the ministry of reconciliation and charged to represent Him—as a national ambassador represents his country—wherever God places them.

context: anyone that's a new creature, anyone that's saved by the grace of God, that we are your ambassadors, that you have committed to us the ministry of reconciliation.
point_summary: Every believer, as a new creature saved by grace, has been entrusted by God with the ministry of reconciliation and the role of ambassador to lost people they alone may reach.

context: anyone that's a new creature, anyone that's saved by the grace of God, that we are your ambassadors, that you have committed to us the ministry of reconciliation.
point_summary: Every believer, as a new creature in Christ, has been entrusted with the ministry of reconciliation and placed as God's ambassador among lost people only they may ever reach.

context: His righteousness has been imputed upon my account, amen? 2 Corinthians 5 verse 21, I am so thankful for that.
point_summary: Christ's righteousness imputed to the believer's account—the standing of perfect justification—is cited as the doctrinal ground for the assurance that sin does not lead to utter defeat.

context: Paul says of himself that he was the chief of sinners. He says in 2 Corinthians 12 that he does not want others to think more highly of him than they should.
point_summary: Even the Apostle Paul, whom believers rightly esteem, cautioned against inflated regard for himself, modeling the humility that marks genuine spiritual maturity in the Christian walk.

context: God said, my grace is sufficient for thee.
point_summary: God's grace declared sufficient to Paul in weakness becomes the ground for the claim that He can sustain believers through opposition and difficulty they cannot face in their own strength.

context: for such are false apostles, deceitful workers, notice what he says that they do, transforming themselves into the apostles of Christ.
point_summary: False apostles and Satan's ministers disguise themselves—transforming into apostles of Christ and angels of light—as part of a deliberate strategy of deception that the enemy employs to infiltrate undetected.

context: And no marvel. For Satan himself is transformed into an angel of light.
point_summary: Satan's strategy of deception—disguising himself and his ministers as servants of light—is cited as the New Testament parallel to enemies who infiltrate undetected before striking.

context: Therefore, it is no great thing if his ministers also be transformed as the ministers of righteousness whose end shall be according to their works.
point_summary: Satan's ministers disguise themselves as servants of righteousness, mirroring Satan's own transformation into an angel of light, so that their destructive work goes undetected until it is too late.

context: Paul would give to us in 2nd Corinthians chapter number 4
point_summary: Satanic blinding of unbelieving minds, not mere intellectual confusion, is identified as the root cause of the people's inability to recognize who Jesus truly is.

context: but if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost.
point_summary: Satan's blinding work, identified in verse 4 as the root cause of spiritual confusion, explains why the crowds in Matthew 16 could not rightly identify Jesus as Messiah and God.

context: in whom the God of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them.
point_summary: Satan's blinding work, identified as the root cause of popular confusion about Jesus's identity, explains why people could acknowledge him as a prophet while missing his true nature as God and Messiah.

context: for though we walk in the flesh, we do not war after the flesh
point_summary: Gehazi's downfall beginning with an entertained thought illustrates the New Testament call to bring every thought into captivity to Christ, warring against mental strongholds through God-given weapons rather than fleshly reasoning.

context: for the weapons of our warfare are not carnal but mighty through God to the pulling down of strongholds
point_summary: Spiritual warfare's weapons are divinely powerful precisely because the real battle begins in the mind, where unguarded thoughts—like Gehazi's covetous scheming—must be brought into captivity to Christ.

context: casting down imaginations and every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God and bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ
point_summary: Sinful actions begin with entertained thoughts, and 2 Corinthians 10:5 commands believers to bring every thought into captivity to the obedience of Christ before those thoughts produce destructive deeds.

context: contrast that was given to us in 2 Corinthians chapter number 3 between law and grace consumed the chapter.
point_summary: The contrast drawn in 2 Corinthians 3 between law and grace establishes that the law condemns and fades in glory, while grace converts, excels in glory, and makes believers like Christ.

context: The apostle Paul started talking about the ministry at the end of chapter number 2.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: as we look at this portion of scripture in 2 Corinthians chapter number 4
point_summary: The "therefore" of 2 Corinthians 4:1 anchors the ministry Paul describes directly to the superior, life-giving, permanent glory of grace established in chapter 3.

context: as we read the opening statement of 2 Corinthians
point_summary: Paul's declaration "therefore, seeing we have this ministry" in chapter 4 flows directly from chapter 3's contrast between law and grace, grounding the minister's confidence in the permanence and life-giving glory of the gospel.

context: the glory of the ministry, that we see, yes, the law, the letter, it killeth, but the spirit, grace, it gives life
point_summary: Chapter 3's contrast between law and gospel grounds the "therefore" of 4:1: the ministry of righteousness that excels in glory—permanent, life-giving, unlike the fading glory of the law—is what Paul says he has received and will not abandon.

context: how God made Jesus to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in Him
point_summary: The "ministration of righteousness" Paul celebrates is grounded in Christ being made sin so that believers might be made the righteousness of God in Him.

context: Therefore, seeing we have this ministry, notice, as we have received mercy, we faint not
point_summary: The "therefore" of 4:1 grounds the minister's perseverance in the surpassing glory of the new-covenant ministry of righteousness received in chapter 3, making mercy—not human strength—the reason one does not faint.

context: but have renounced the hidden things of dishonesty, not walking in craftiness, nor handling the word of God deceitfully, but by manifestation of the truth, commending ourselves to every man's conscience in the sight of God
point_summary: Faithful gospel ministry, grounded in received mercy, renounces all deception and handles God's word honestly, commending itself openly to every conscience before God.

context: But if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost
point_summary: When the gospel is hidden, it is hidden specifically to the lost — those whose minds the god of this world has blinded against the light of Christ.

context: in whom the God of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them
point_summary: Satan blinds unbelievers specifically to prevent the light of the glorious gospel of Christ from shining unto them, making lostness a condition of spiritual blindness rather than mere ignorance.

context: For we preach not ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord, and ourselves your servants for Jesus' sake
point_summary: The gospel ministry is defined not by self-promotion but by proclaiming Christ Jesus as Lord, with the preacher serving others for Jesus' sake.

context: For God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness, hath shined in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ
point_summary: The same God who commanded light to shine at creation has shined in believers' hearts, making the knowledge of God's glory — seen in Christ — the treasure carried in frail, human vessels.

context: Notice verse 7, but we have, we have this treasure in earthen vessels, that the excellency of the power may be of God and not of us
point_summary: The surpassing power of the gospel ministry belongs to God alone, not to the frail, clay-like human vessels who carry it.

context: therefore seeing, we have this ministry
point_summary: Ministry received through mercy carries with it a weighty responsibility—Paul's "therefore seeing we have this ministry" frames gospel stewardship as obligation, not privilege to boast in.

context: We understand that he laid that out in 2 Corinthians chapter number one.
point_summary: Paul's apostolic calling, established in 2 Corinthians 1, grounds the plural "we have this ministry" in 4:1, extending gospel responsibility beyond apostles to every believer.

reference_text: second Corinthians chapter four and verse number 16
context: He'll say again in second Corinthians chapter four and verse number 16, for which cause we faint not. But though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed day by day
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we've renounced the hidden things of dishonesty, not walking in craftiness nor handling the word of God deceitfully, but by manifestation of the truth, commending ourselves to every man's conscience in the sight of God.
point_summary: Paul's renunciation of craftiness and deceitful handling of Scripture, set against the false teachers' methods, illustrates the life-and-death seriousness that must govern gospel ministry.

context: for we are not as many which corrupt the word of God, but as of sincerity, but as of God and the sight of God speak we in Christ.

context: if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost

context: And if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost.
point_summary: The hiddenness of the gospel is a crisis of urgency, because a veiled gospel means lost people remain lost — and the saved bear responsibility to carry it to them.

context: We find that Paul says, if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost.
point_summary: The hiddenness of the gospel in 2 Corinthians 4:3 is not a flaw in the message but a condition of the hearers—the lost—who urgently need saved people to bring it to them.

context: Notice verse six, but God, he references all the way back to Genesis 1 and creation, but God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness, hath shined in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.

context: In whom the God of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them.

context: In verse number four, it said that the God of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not.

context: but we have this treasure in earthen vessels that the excellency of the power may be of God and not of us

context: And that He died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves but unto Him which died for them and rose again.

context: Wherefore, verse 16, henceforth know we no man after the flesh, yea, though we have known Christ after the flesh, yet now henceforth know we Him no more.

context: Therefore, if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. Old things are passed away. Behold, all things are become new.

context: And all things are of God who hath, notice what it says, reconciled us to Himself, how? By Jesus Christ, and hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation.

context: for He hath made Him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in Him.

context: ...for He hath made Him to be sin for us, who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in Him.

context: Think about the Apostle Paul in 2 Corinthians 12. He's dealing with infirmity, he's dealing with a difficulty,

context: And the Lord's answer was what? My grace is sufficient for thee. context: for we preach not ourselves, it is not about us

context: Therefore seeing we have this ministry as we have received mercy, we faint not.

context: it is hid to them that are lost.

context: while we look not at those things which are temporal, but at those things which are eternal
point_summary: Eternal perspective reframes present suffering as "light affliction" and momentary when measured against the weight of what is unseen and everlasting.

context: If any man be in Christ, he's a new creature. The Bible says, old things are passed away, behold, all things are become new.
point_summary: Regeneration produces observable transformation — old things passing away and all things becoming new — serving as evidence that the Holy Spirit has genuinely indwelt and changed the believer at salvation.

context: Blessed be God, even the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies and the God of all comfort.
point_summary: God's title as "the Father of mercies and the God of all comfort" grounds the command to give thanks in every circumstance, because suffering becomes the means by which He equips believers to comfort others.

context: God might allow you to go through an experience today in your life, and it may not be for a number of years that you understand why God was allowing you to go through that experience and how God comforted you in that situation, and then God would use you to be able to help someone else.
point_summary: God comforts believers in their trials so that, in time, they may use that same comfort to minister to others going through similar difficulties.

context: Paul said this, today is the day of salvation
point_summary: The urgency of "today is the day of salvation" grounds the appeal that Christ's return has not yet come precisely because God is long-suffering, unwilling that any should perish before trusting Him.

context: the God of this world hath blinded their minds, lest the light of the glorious Gospel should shine in unto them
point_summary: Satan's blinding of unbelieving minds (2 Cor. 4:4) is cited as the urgent reason believers labor to get the gospel and printed Scriptures to those still in darkness before Christ's return.

context: Knowing the terror of the Lord, what is it, 2 Corinthians chapter number 5?
point_summary: Awareness of the coming tribulation's terror should motivate believers toward evangelism, as Paul's phrase "the terror of the Lord" grounds the urgency to reach the lost before the rapture.

context: for we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ that everyone may receive the things done in his body according to that he hath done, whether it be good or bad.
point_summary: The certainty that all will appear before Christ's judgment seat — and the terror awaiting the lost — is cited as the motivating force compelling believers to persuade men and pursue souls with urgency.

context: for the love of Christ constraineth us because we thus judge that if one died for all, then we're all dead.
point_summary: Christ's love, which compelled Paul to persuade men by knowing the terror of the Lord, is cited as the motivating force behind soul-winning urgency in light of Christ's return.

context: and that he died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which died for them and rose again.
point_summary: Christ died for all so that believers would no longer live for themselves but for him—a truth the sermon uses to ground the church's evangelistic responsibility in light of his return.

context: therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. Old things are passed away, behold all things are become new.
point_summary: Being "in Christ" identifies the saved person as a new creature — the very ones Paul calls ambassadors for Christ, compelled by His love to persuade the lost before His return.

context: now then we are ambassadors for Christ.
point_summary: Believers, identified as new creatures in Christ (v. 17), are therefore ambassadors for Christ (v. 20), a role the sermon uses to press the urgency of soul-winning in light of Christ's return.

context: for he hath made him, Jesus Christ, to be sin for us, who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.
point_summary: Christ's sinlessness was imputed to believers in the great exchange of the cross, grounding the saved person's identity as an ambassador and motivating evangelism before Christ's return.

reference_text: He who knew no sin, He was made sin for us.
context: He who knew no sin, He was made sin for us. The innocent died for the guilty.
point_summary: Christ's sinlessness made Him the only acceptable substitute, so that His righteousness—what the sermon calls "imputed righteousness"—is credited to the believer's account in place of their own unacceptable works.

context: You read 2 Corinthians chapter number 1, and you find that to be declared for us. He is the God of all comfort, who comforteth us in all of our infirmities.
point_summary: God is identified in 2 Corinthians 1 as the God of all comfort who comforts in every trouble, a truth the sermon connects to Scripture itself as the instrument of that comfort.

context: Paul, he besought the Lord thrice for this thing, that thorn in the flesh.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: God said, my grace is sufficient for thee.
point_summary: God answers prayer persistently and specifically, though his answer may differ from what is sought, as Paul's repeated plea for thorn-removal was met with sufficiency of grace rather than removal.

context: turn over to the book of 2 Corinthians, if you will, in chapter number 11, and I want you to note quickly some of what Paul records for the church in Corinth that he had suffered
point_summary: Paul's catalog of sufferings in 2 Corinthians 11 is cited to show the concrete hardships against which his request for prayer in 1 Thessalonians 5:25 must be understood.

context: The Bible says, are they Hebrews? So am I. Are they Israelites? So am I. Are they the seed of Abraham? So am I.
point_summary: Paul's credentials and catalog of sufferings in 2 Corinthians 11 are cited to show the extreme affliction and spiritual warfare he endured while preaching, establishing why he urgently needed the church's prayer support.

context: Are they ministers of Christ? I speak as a fool. I am more. And then notice, he begins to get into this list. In labors more abundant, in stripes above measure, in prisons more frequent, in deaths oft.
point_summary: Paul's catalog of sufferings in 2 Corinthians 11:23 is cited to show the severity of affliction and spiritual warfare he endured in ministry, establishing why intercessory prayer from the churches was so urgently needed.

context: Of the Jews, five times received I 40 stripes, save one.
point_summary: Paul's repeated endurance of thirty-nine lashes—a beating calibrated to bring a man to the edge of death without killing him—illustrates the severe physical cost he bore for preaching the Word, underscoring why he urgently sought the church's prayers.

context: thrice was I beaten with rods. Once was I stoned. Thrice I suffered shipwreck. A night and a day I've been in the deep.
point_summary: Paul's catalogue of beatings, stonings, and shipwrecks in 2 Corinthians 11:25 is marshaled as evidence of the relentless physical opposition he endured for preaching the word, grounding his later plea for prayer in lived, documented suffering.

context: In journeyings often, in perils of waters, in perils of robbers, in perils by my own countrymen, in perils by the heathen, in perils in the city, in perils in the wilderness, in perils in the sea, in perils among false brethren,
point_summary: Paul's extensive catalog of sufferings in 2 Corinthians 11 — beatings, shipwrecks, and perils of every kind — establishes the real and costly spiritual warfare behind his urgent request for prayer in 2 Thessalonians 3:2.

context: in weariness and painfulness, in watchings often, in hunger and thirst, in fastings often, in cold and nakedness.
point_summary: Paul's extensive catalog of physical sufferings — beatings, shipwreck, hunger, cold — establishes the brutal cost of gospel ministry that made his request for prayer both urgent and credible.

context: beside those things that are without, that which cometh upon me daily, the care of all the churches.
point_summary: After cataloguing beatings, shipwrecks, and constant peril, Paul identifies the daily burden that surpasses them all: the care of all the churches.

context: Paul he prayed thrice that that thorn in the flesh would depart from him
point_summary: God sometimes chooses to develop believers through suffering rather than deliver them from it, as illustrated by Paul's unanswered threefold prayer and Christ's response that His grace is sufficient.

context: what did Jesus say my grace is sufficient for thee my grace is sufficient for thee
point_summary: God's refusal to remove Paul's thorn illustrates that hallowing God's name requires submitting to His will even when He chooses to develop us through a trial rather than deliver us from it.

context: If any man be in Christ, he's a new creature
point_summary: Regeneration—becoming a new creature in Christ—is the foundation of salvation, but the sermon distinguishes it from revival, which is the awakening a born-again believer still needs.

context: When I got saved, the Bible says in Christ, he's a new creature. Old things are passed away. Behold, all things are become new.
point_summary: New creation in Christ is the basis for sanctification: the indwelling Holy Spirit convicts the believer away from former sins precisely because old things have passed and all things have become new.

reference_text: the God of this world, Satan, has blinded the minds of them that believe not
context: the God of this world, Satan, has blinded the minds of them that believe not.
point_summary: Satan's blinding work (2 Cor. 4:4) is invoked to explain why a culture that suppresses God cannot see its own accountability, following the pattern of Romans 1.

context: if any man be in Christ, he's a new creature. Old things are passed away, behold, all things are become new.
point_summary: Saul's dramatic conversion from persecutor to preacher illustrates that the only remedy for humanity's heart problem is gospel-wrought transformation—becoming a new creature in Christ—not reformation or social programs.

context: Notice what he says over in 2 Corinthians chapter number 5
point_summary: Dying holds no terror for the believer because, as Paul declares in 2 Corinthians 5:8, to be absent from the body is to be present with Christ—a confidence that removes all fear of death.

context: we are confident, I say, and willing rather to be absent from the body and to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: Confidence in the believer's death rests on Paul's assurance that departing the body means immediate presence with the Lord, removing all fear of dying.

context: in the same passage of Scripture in 2 Corinthians chapter 5 and verse number 8, when Paul says, we are confident, I say, and willing rather to be absent from the body and to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Paul's confidence that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord grounds the call to help others prepare for death by sharing the gospel.

context: For sake of time, we'll pick it up in verse 18. But notice what the Bible says here in 2 Corinthians 5 and verse number 18. The Bible says, and all things are of God who hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Reconciliation through Christ—the means by which estranged sinners are brought into favor with God—grounds the believer's commission to a ministry of reconciliation toward those who have not yet prepared to die.

context: and hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation
point_summary: Reconciliation—sinners brought from enmity into favor with God through Jesus Christ—is not only a gift received but a ministry entrusted to believers, who are sent as ambassadors urging others to be reconciled to God.

context: that God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto himself, not imputing their trespasses unto them
point_summary: God's act of reconciling the world to himself through Christ carries with it a commission—believers are entrusted with the word of reconciliation as ambassadors pleading for sinners to be reconciled to God.

context: Now in verse number 19, he says, and hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation.
point_summary: God's act of reconciling the world to himself through Christ, not imputing their trespasses, grounds the believer's commission to carry both the ministry and the word of reconciliation as his ambassadors.

context: And then he says this in verse number 20, now then, we are ambassadors for Christ.
point_summary: Believers, entrusted with both the ministry and word of reconciliation, are Christ's ambassadors—representatives of a heavenly citizenship commissioned to call those around them to be reconciled to God.

context: For he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.
point_summary: Reconciliation with God through Christ—who was made sin so believers might become the righteousness of God—grounds the believer's identity as an ambassador commissioned with the ministry and word of reconciliation.

context: I'm mindful of the truth in 2 Corinthians 5 and verse 17.
point_summary: Regeneration produces such a thorough transformation that Paul's own testimony—from persecutor to preacher—illustrates what it means to become a new creature in Christ.

context: I want you to think about 2 Corinthians 5 and verse 17. Now, the Apostle Paul is the human penman for the book of 2 Corinthians as well. And I want you to think about this. The Bible says, therefore, if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. Old things are passed away. Behold, all things are become new.
point_summary: Familiar as a quotation, 2 Corinthians 5:17 gains depth when read as Paul's personal testimony—the man who wrote "old things are passed away" had himself lived that transformation.

context: It says that he is a new creature. I mean, can you imagine Paul writing these words? Old things are passed away. Behold, all things are become new. And as he would write these words to encourage the believers in Corinth to be preserved for us today, I just wonder the way in which his mind would flood with thoughts and with appreciation for the work that Christ did in him. He was a new creature. I mean, he was changed as one person would say, from the bottom of his feet to the top of his head. I mean, he was changed. There was evidence of his conversion. We find that as he would be used of God to pen these words in 2 Corinthians 5 and verse 17, his own life testified of the message that was being sent to this church in Corinth. That he was in Christ and he was a new creature. And that old things were passed away.
point_summary: Paul's authorship of 2 Corinthians 5:17 is presented as personally autobiographical — he himself was the new creature he described, his own transformed life testifying to the very truth he wrote.

reference_text: second Corinthians chapter number five...verse number 17
context: we visited this passage just a few moments ago and we looked at verse number 17
point_summary: Regeneration produces observable transformation — being "in Christ" makes one a genuinely new creature, not merely a more religious person, as Paul's Damascus-road conversion visibly demonstrated.

context: for the love of Christ constraineth us, it holds us together, it controls us. Why? Because God must judge that if one died for all, then we're all dead
point_summary: Christ's love as a constraining force—controlling and holding together the believer—is what drove Paul's effective ministry, compelling him to live not for himself but for the risen Christ.

context: and that he died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which died for them and rose again
point_summary: Christ's death demands that those who live no longer live for themselves but for him—a principle Paul did not merely teach the Corinthians but personally embodied as the driving motivation behind his effective ministry.

context: He had testified in second Corinthians to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: Death holds no dread for the believer because to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord, making departure to Christ the far better gain.

context: Second Corinthians chapter number five, Second Corinthians chapter number five, and I'll let you know that we're going to, that's the text that I'll be preaching from this morning
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The Bible says this in verse number eight, we are confident, I say, and willing, rather to be absent from the body and to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: Bodily death for the believer means immediate presence with the Lord, a truth Paul's words ground as the basis for confident, joyful anticipation of eternity.

context: then we'll get back to 2 Corinthians 5, dig into the passage of Scripture this morning.
point_summary: God's eternal plan—climaxing in His personal return, the resurrection of the dead in Christ, and unending dwelling with God—is not human speculation but divine revelation recorded in Scripture.

context: Turn back over to 2 Corinthians 5. Sometimes we live in this earth and this world, and we can get so caught up with the things of this world.
point_summary: God's eternal plan, revealed in Scripture, is for his children to dwell with him forever — a perspective that guards believers against discouragement amid earthly distractions.

context: In 2 Corinthians chapter number five, if you're taking notes, number one, I want you to notice with me what God has revealed to his people. Notice with me, as we look at this portion of scripture, what God has revealed to his people. In fact, as we've looked at all of those other portions of scripture, we find that those portions of scripture support, as we look here at this thought of what God has revealed to us, I'm so thankful for God's revelation. In verse number one, notice how Paul begins. Notice the first three words, for we know.
point_summary: Paul's opening phrase "for we know" establishes that the believer's confidence about death and eternity rests on divine revelation and certain knowledge, not mere hope or opinion.

context: for we know. That if our earthly house of this tabernacle were dissolved
point_summary: Paul's opening words "for we know" are read as divine revelation granting believers certain, assured confidence—not mere hope or opinion—that death dissolves only the earthly body while a heavenly, eternal dwelling awaits.

context: he that hath wrought us for the self, same thing as God, who also hath given unto us the earnest of the spirit.
point_summary: God's gift of the Spirit to believers is identified as the "earnest" — the down payment guaranteeing that mortality will ultimately be swallowed up not by death but by eternal life.

context: Therefore, we are always confident, knowing that whilst we are at home in the body, we are absent from the Lord.
point_summary: Grounded in the certainty of a resurrection body and eternal life, the believer's present separation from the Lord while inhabiting the physical body is the very basis of Paul's repeated, emphatic confidence.

context: For we walk by faith, not by sight.
point_summary: Walking by faith rather than sight is presented as the present-tense posture of believers who are bodily separated from the Lord but confident in the eternal dwelling awaiting them.

context: We are confident, I say, and willing, rather to be absent from the body than to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: Confidence in the believer's resurrection and eternal dwelling grounds Paul's repeated, deliberate assertion that departing the body means being present with the Lord.

context: Wherefore, we labor, that whether present or absent, we may be accepted of him.
point_summary: Whether present in the body or absent from it, the believer's singular aim is to be accepted of God — an ambition grounded in the threefold confidence Paul expresses across these nine verses.

context: we know that we have a building of God. We know that this house, this building of God and house that's not made with hands, that's eternal in the heavens.
point_summary: The believer's confidence rests on knowing that the present earthly body will be replaced by an eternal heavenly dwelling not made with hands, so that mortality is swallowed up by life, not death.

context: knowing that whilst we're at home in the body, we're absent from the Lord.
point_summary: Bodily life in this present age constitutes absence from the Lord, and Paul's repeated confidence in this fact—stated three times within nine verses—grounds the believer's assurance against the fear of death.

context: we are confident, I say, and willing rather to be absent from the body and to be present with the Lord.
point_summary: Paul's repeated use of "confident" across verses 6 and 8 underscores that the believer's willingness to depart the body and be present with the Lord rests on assurance, not presumption.

context: we are confident, I say, and willing rather to be absent from the body and to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Believers who have trusted Christ can face death without fear, holding the confident assurance that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord.

context: now he that hath wrought us for the selfsame thing as God, notice what he says at the end of verse number five, who also hath given unto us the earnest of the spirit
point_summary: The Holy Spirit given at salvation is God's "earnest"—a down payment guaranteeing the believer's future inheritance, paralleled in Ephesians 1 with the same term.

context: And here we find that in 2 Corinthians five, Paul says that we have received, that God has given unto us the earnest of the spirit.
point_summary: The indwelling Holy Spirit, given as the "earnest" of our inheritance, shifts the believer's perspective from temporal, self-centered ambition to confident, eternity-grounded living.

context: When we notice within the passage that's here, 2 Corinthians chapter number five, we notice a couple of things.
point_summary: Divine revelation of the believer's eternal destiny—culminating in permanent presence with God—should produce a longing for heaven expressed in the groaning Paul describes in verses two and four.

context: Paul is writing to these folks there in the city of Corinth in 2 Corinthians 5
point_summary: Paul's teaching in 2 Corinthians 5 produces in believers a longing for heaven that loosens their grip on earthly things, because they understand God still has a purpose for them until He calls them home.

context: we are confident, I say, and willing rather to be absent from the body and to be present with the Lord
point_summary: The confidence of being absent from the body and present with the Lord (v. 8) is paired immediately with the obligation to labor for Christ's acceptance, grounding present holy living in the certainty of future accountability at the judgment seat.

context: wherefore we labor that whether present or absent, notice this phrase, we may be accepted of him
point_summary: The confidence of being "present with the Lord" (v. 8) drives a corresponding obligation: believers must labor now to be accepted of Him, with the coming judgment seat of Christ giving that ambition its eternal weight.

context: The question here in second Corinthians five is not whether I'm going to heaven or not.
point_summary: Assurance of salvation through Christ's finished work is settled, but Paul's concern in 2 Corinthians 5 shifts to whether believers will be found pleasing—accepted—before the Lord at His appearing.

context: the Bible says today is the day of salvation.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: ...that whether present or absent, that we would be accepted of him...
point_summary: The certainty of Christ's appearing—whether at death or His return—calls believers to daily diligence, living so as to be found accepted by Him and unashamed at His coming.
Christians are called ambassadors for Christ whose purpose is to beseech others to be reconciled to God, a role the sermon frames as both a privilege and a mission.
