context: In verse number 9 of 2 Corinthians 5, the apostle Paul said, Wherefore we labor, that whether present or absent, we may be accepted of him.
point_summary: Living to please God is the Christian's ongoing labor — not to earn salvation, which is already settled at the cross, but because believers are now children of their heavenly Father.

context: he died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which died for them and rose again
point_summary: Christ's death obligates believers to live not for themselves but for him—a motivation the sermon presents as the proper response to standing before the judgment seat.

context: For he, the he that begins this verse of Scripture speaking of God the Father, hath made him, speaking of the Lord Jesus Christ, to be sin for us. To be sin for us. Notice this, who knew no sin. That we might be made the righteousness of God in him.
point_summary: God the Father made the sinless Christ to be sin so that believers might receive the righteousness of God — a transaction that transforms identity, priorities, and the whole of life.

context: And the Bible says that He made Him to be sin for us, who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in Him.
point_summary: God the Father made the sinless Son to be sin so that believers might receive the righteousness of God in Him — a perfect substitutionary exchange requiring Christ's absolute holiness as its basis.

context: we begin to understand this wonderful truth as we look at it in 2 Corinthians 5 and verse 21, for he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.
point_summary: God completed the transaction of redemption by making the sinless Christ to bear sin so that believers might receive the righteousness of God, the only means by which mankind could be saved.

context: for you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ that though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor, why? So that we through his poverty might be rich.
point_summary: Christ completed the great transaction of redemption by voluntarily leaving heaven's glory—though eternally rich—to become poor, so that sinners might be made rich through his poverty.

reference_text: He hath made him to be sin for us
context: He hath made him to be sin for us. He bore the sin of the world once for all.
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all sacrifice on the cross bore the sin of the world, satisfying God's demands and proving the substitutionary atonement acceptable to God, accomplishing what sinners could never do for themselves.

context: the second Corinthians chapter five in a single chapter.
point_summary: Twice within 2 Corinthians 5 alone, the Bema Seat judgment is raised, pressing the urgency that earthly opportunities to serve Christ end at death and cannot be recovered.

context: for he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.
point_summary: Christ's sinlessness, established in 2 Corinthians 5:21, is confirmed by 1 Peter 2's parallel witness that he "did no sin," making him the qualified substitute who bore our sins on the tree.

context: This truth of 2 Corinthians 5.21 is so important for us as God's people that we understand the basis of the transaction.
point_summary: Christ's substitutionary death is identified as the necessary "basis of the transaction," because sin must be paid for and only the sinless Lamb could take humanity's place before God.

context: he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: Imputed righteousness—Christ's sinless record credited to the believer's account in exchange for the believer's sin—is identified as the central blessing of the transaction described in this verse.

context: as we noticed earlier in the book of 2 Corinthians, as we've studied and looked through this book and studied verse by verse, we find that it was in chapter number 3 that there was that great contrast between the law and grace.
point_summary: Second Corinthians 3 is invoked as prior groundwork establishing that the law kills and condemns rather than imparts life or righteousness, reinforcing why imputed righteousness must come through Christ alone.

context: for he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.
point_summary: The righteousness described in 2 Corinthians 5:21 cannot come through law-keeping; if it could, Christ's death would be meaningless, making the cross the only ground of imputed righteousness.

context: Remember 2 Corinthians 4? In whom the God of this world hath blinded. Why? Why is he blinding their minds? Lest the light of the glorious gospel should shine in unto them
point_summary: Satan blinds the minds of the lost specifically to prevent the gospel's light from penetrating, keeping people trapped in the delusion that self-produced righteousness can save them.

context: But 2 Corinthians 5.21 settles all of it. Because the only way to get the righteousness of God is to accept the Lord Jesus Christ, your personal savior, recognizing that he took your sin and he offers you his righteousness.
point_summary: 2 Corinthians 5:21 settles the futility of law-keeping by establishing that God's righteousness comes only through receiving Christ, who exchanged our sin for his righteousness—the "great transaction."

context: For he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him.
point_summary: Christ's imputed righteousness, received through faith in him alone apart from works, is the completed transaction of Calvary — "paid in full" — by which sinners are made the righteousness of God.

context: the very next text in 2 Corinthians 6, Paul says today is the day of salvation
point_summary: Cited as the preacher closes the sermon, 2 Corinthians 6:2 grounds the urgent appeal that salvation must not be deferred, because today—not later—is the appointed day.

context: if any man be in Christ, he's a new creature. Old things are passed away, behold, all things are become new
point_summary: Salvation is a definitive moment of transformation—not a process—evidenced by the new-creature reality of 2 Corinthians 5:17, illustrated through John's immediate response to Christ's call.

context: if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature
point_summary: Genuine conversion produces fruit, evidenced in transformed behavior, because the one in Christ is a new creature created unto good works.

context: for he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: The divine exchange described in 2 Corinthians 5:21—Christ made sin so believers are made the righteousness of God—is presented as the miraculous transaction that makes salvation possible entirely apart from human works.

context: the little G, God of this world, has blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel should shine unto them
point_summary: Satan's blinding of unbelieving minds, cited from 2 Corinthians 4:4, is presented as evidence that spiritual deception is real and accounts for humanity's bondage to false teaching.

context: In 2 Corinthians chapter number four

context: later in chapter 10, the Bible talks about how Satan transforms himself into an angel of light

context: the things which are seen are temporal, but the things which are not seen are eternal

context: Notice in verse number 20, in 2 Corinthians 12, in verse 20, the Bible says this, 'For I fear, lest when I come, I shall not find you such as I would, and that I should be found unto you such as you would not, lest there be debates, envyings, wraths, strifes, backbitings, whisperings, swellings, tumults.'

context: as we look at verse 20 and 21 of 2 Corinthians 12

context: for he God hath made him Jesus to be sin for us who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God in him

context: Lest I be exalted above measure

context: he says, I seek not yours but you in verse 14
