context: all scripture is given by inspiration of God
point_summary: Paul's closing verses to the Corinthians are framed as a farewell summary of the entire epistle, issuing final instruction on two inseparable concerns: behavior and belief.

context: for we are glad when we are weak, and ye are strong, and this also we wish, even your perfection
point_summary: Paul's wish for the Corinthians' "perfection" in verse 9 carries the meaning of spiritual maturity and completeness, anticipating the identical call to "be perfect" issued in verse 11.

context: be perfect
point_summary: Paul's command "be perfect" in verse 11 is not a salvation formula but a call to spiritual maturity and completeness, addressed specifically to those already born again.

context: as we've studied through the book of 2 Corinthians, the truth is mentioned throughout the book
point_summary: Paul's call to "be perfect" in 2 Corinthians 13:11 means spiritual maturity and completeness, grounded in the believer's position in Christ as confirmed by Colossians 2.

context: he says, be perfect. Take that which has been given and apply it in your life. Be mature spiritually. He says this in verse number 11, back in 2nd Corinthians 13, be of good comfort. Be of good comfort.
point_summary: Paul's command "be perfect" in 13:11 is inseparable from personal responsibility—prayer for spiritual maturity cannot substitute for an individual's own choice to read, apply, and obey God's Word.

context: we find here in 2nd Corinthians 13 that he says be of good comfort
point_summary: Paul's closing charge "be of good comfort" calls believers to actively console the faint-hearted, a mutual ministry grounded in the comfort God himself supplies through the Holy Spirit.

context: God is the God of all comfort in verse number 3
point_summary: God's comfort received in tribulation is meant to flow outward, equipping believers to comfort others who are faint-hearted and overwhelmed by life's adversities.

context: who comforteth us in all our tribulation, but then notice that we may be able to comfort them which are in any trouble by the comfort wherewith we ourselves are comforted of God
point_summary: God's comfort received in tribulation is not merely personal but is meant to flow outward, equipping believers to comfort others who face similar trouble.

context: keep your place in 2nd Corinthians
point_summary: Paul's call to "be of one mind" in 2 Corinthians 13 is grounded not in agreeing to disagree but in every believer submitting to Scripture, since shared agreement with God's Word is what produces genuine unity.

context: all of those things that we looked at through the book of 2 Corinthians
point_summary: Unity among believers is grounded not in personal opinion but in shared submission to what God has said, which Paul frames as the pathway to living in peace.

context: Remember in the book of 2 Corinthians, we understand that we've been given the ministry of reconciliation
point_summary: The ministry of reconciliation entrusted to believers (2 Cor. 5:18) grounds the call to "live in peace," since those commissioned to reconcile God and sinful humanity must themselves embody that reconciliation.

context: we looked in 2 Corinthians 5 and noticed all of that with regard to the ministry
point_summary: The ministry of reconciliation entrusted to believers in 2 Corinthians 5 loses credibility when those same believers remain unreconciled in their own relationships with family and church members.

context: be perfect, be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace
point_summary: Paul's four closing imperatives in 2 Corinthians 13:11 are read as a summary charge to godly living, with the promised consequence that the God of love and peace will be with those who obey.

context: God resisteth the proud. We talked about that earlier, right? In second Corinthians chapter 12.
point_summary: God's resistance of the proud (referenced from 2 Corinthians 12) is distinguished from his never-leaving promise, showing that while salvation is secure, sweet fellowship with God can be hindered by ungodly living.

context: be perfect, be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace and the God of love and peace shall be with you
point_summary: Paul's closing command to "live in peace" in 2 Corinthians 13:11 is tied to the promise that the God of love and peace will be present in sweet fellowship—a communion distinct from the security of salvation itself.

context: the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the communion of the Holy Ghost be with you all
point_summary: The trinitarian benediction of v. 14 is read as an explicit, early-church affirmation of the Trinity, with each person—Father, Son, and Holy Spirit—distinctly named and assigned a characteristic gift.

context: for ye know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor, that ye through his poverty might be rich
point_summary: Christ's self-impoverishment in the incarnation—becoming poor though he was rich—is the concrete content behind Paul's closing benediction phrase "the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ" in 2 Corinthians 13:14.

context: though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor
point_summary: Christ's self-impoverishment in the Incarnation—leaving heavenly glory to take on human flesh—is identified as the theological ground of the grace named in 2 Corinthians 13:14, with the point that only a fully human Christ could atone for human sin.

context: the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the communion of the Holy Ghost
point_summary: The threefold benediction of 2 Corinthians 13:14 is read as a compressed summary of Christian belief: the incarnation in Christ's grace, redemption through his blood, and the Spirit's ongoing fellowship with believers.

context: be perfect. Notice, be of good comfort. He says to them, to be of one mind, to have that unity, to live in peace
point_summary: Paul's closing commands in 2 Corinthians 13:11—be perfect, be of good comfort, be of one mind, live in peace—are presented as active obligations requiring personal application of truth, not merely the result of prayer.

context: as we finish up the book of 2 Corinthians
point_summary: Paul's closing charge in 2 Corinthians calls believers to both right behavior and sound doctrine, insisting that doctrine matters and must not be compromised for the sake of unity.

context: His grace is sufficient
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: In 2 Corinthians chapter number four
point_summary: Maintaining an eternal perspective—fixing one's gaze on unseen, eternal glory rather than present circumstances—is the biblical corrective to covetousness and the key to contentment amid financial fluctuation.

context: God hath made him Jesus to be sin for us who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: Salvation cannot be purchased or earned through works or religion, because righteousness comes only through God's imputation of Christ's sinless nature to the believer, as quoted directly from 2 Corinthians 5:21.

context: Second Corinthians chapter number 12
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: As we come to Second Corinthians chapter number 12
point_summary: Serving as a chapter-level reference point, 2 Corinthians 12 is introduced here as the sermon's text within a year-long study of Second Corinthians, anchored by the sufficiency of God's grace amid trials.

context: we've studied the last couple of Sunday mornings in Second Corinthians chapter number 11
point_summary: Boasting, identified as both the root and the result of pride, characterizes the false teachers Paul exposes in 2 Corinthians 11 as context for his own reluctant self-defense before the Corinthians.

context: Paul begins the text of Scripture by requesting that the believers in Corinth bear with him for a little while. He says, bear with me a little in my folly
point_summary: Boasting among the false teachers at Corinth—rooted in and resulting in pride—is the backdrop against which Paul's request for patient hearing in chapter 11 is introduced.

reference_text: if I must needs glory, I will glory of the things which concern mine infirmities
context: Paul said, if I must needs glory, I will glory of the things which concern mine infirmities
point_summary: When compelled to boast, Paul chose to glory only in his infirmities, redirecting any honor away from himself and toward the Lord Jesus Christ.

context: 2 Corinthians chapter number 12
point_summary: Paul's forced defense of his ministry against boasting false teachers frames the entire passage as a study in contrasts between pride-driven boasting and the humility that redirects all glory to Christ.

context: We'll look at it here in 2 Corinthians 12
point_summary: A proper view of God's greatness is the foundation of humility, because seeing Him as high and holy corrects the self-exaltation that Scripture identifies as the root of all contention.

context: it is not expedient for me, doubtless to glory. I will come to visions and revelations of the Lord
point_summary: Paul's extraordinary spiritual experiences — visions and revelations of the Lord — are introduced as the very context that makes the danger of self-exaltation, and therefore the necessity of humility, most acute.

context: I knew a man in Christ about fourteen years ago, whether in the body I cannot tell or whether out of the body I cannot tell, God knoweth
point_summary: Paul's extraordinary heavenly experience—being caught up to the third heaven—serves as the backdrop against which his deliberate refusal to boast in himself becomes the defining mark of true humility.

context: he was caught up into paradise and heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter
point_summary: Even the most extraordinary divine encounter — being caught up into paradise and hearing unspeakable words — gave Paul no grounds for self-glory, only deeper humility.

context: Of such in one will I glory, yet of myself I will not glory, but in mine infirmities
point_summary: Paul's refusal to boast in his own extraordinary experiences—choosing instead to glory only in his infirmities—illustrates that self-glorying, however warranted, is ultimately foolishness before God.

context: For though I would desire to glory, I shall not be a fool
point_summary: Though every person desires to glory in themselves, Paul calls such self-glorying foolish, choosing instead to restrain boasting so others will not think more of him than what they actually see.

context: for I will say the truth, but now I forbear, lest any man should think of me above that which he seeth me to be, or that he heareth of me
point_summary: Glorying in oneself is foolishness, and Paul restrains self-promotion specifically so no one will think of him beyond what they can actually observe.

context: Through the abundance of the revelations, there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, the messenger of Satan to buffet me, lest I should be exalted above measure
point_summary: God's purpose in giving Paul the thorn in the flesh was to prevent him from being exalted above measure through the abundance of his revelations, making humility a divine priority, not merely a personal virtue.

context: For this thing I besought the Lord thrice, but that it might depart from me
point_summary: God's refusal to remove Paul's thorn—despite three earnest requests—served as a divine safeguard against pride, keeping Paul from being exalted above measure.

context: My grace is sufficient for thee, for my strength is made perfect in weakness. Most gladly, therefore, will I rather glory in my infirmities, that the power of Christ may rest upon me
point_summary: God's answer to Paul's repeated prayer for relief was not removal of the thorn but the assurance that divine strength is perfected precisely in human weakness, making humility the condition through which Christ's power rests on a believer.

context: Therefore I take pleasure in infirmities, in reproaches, in necessities, in persecutions, in distresses for Christ's sake. For when I am weak, then am I strong
point_summary: Genuine strength in weakness is the sermon's controlling paradox: Paul's contentment in infirmities, reproaches, and distresses becomes the basis for prioritizing humility over self-glorying.

context: As you read verses 1 through 5, which I read by way of introduction, you find that the Apostle Paul is informing these believers in Corinth about these visions and revelations.
point_summary: Paul's visions and revelations, introduced in verse 1, serve as the backdrop against which his humility is measured — he possessed extraordinary spiritual experiences yet refused to let others esteem him beyond what was warranted.

context: we find that the Apostle Paul will say in verse number 7 that there was an abundance of revelations
point_summary: God's gift of extraordinary revelations to Paul was itself the occasion requiring the counterbalancing thorn, underscoring that an abundance of divine privilege carries a corresponding danger of pride.

context: we find that in verses 2 through 4, he begins to speak about this specific example
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: there are times in which we look at 2 Corinthians chapter 12 and we think that Paul had some infirmity
point_summary: Paul's humility is seen not only in how he bore suffering but in his deliberate restraint about extraordinary visions he had kept private for over fourteen years.

context: when he gives a specific example at the end of 2 Corinthians 11
point_summary: Paul's humility is grounded in a proven track record of divine deliverance, beginning with his escape from Damascus—the first of many times God proved faithful—which anchors his confidence in God rather than himself.

context: he was caught up in the third heaven, God's dwelling place
point_summary: Paul's reference to a vision received fourteen years prior establishes that his apostolic credentials rested on a long, proven track record of divine encounter, not recent or convenient claims.

context: let me talk to you about revelations and visions
point_summary: Paul's reference to visions and revelations fourteen years prior is presented as a deliberate, measured defense of his apostolic authority against false teachers undermining his ministry in Corinth.

context: he was caught up into paradise and heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter
point_summary: Paul's heavenly experience included words so sacred they were inexpressible and unlawful to repeat, a detail the sermon uses to contrast authentic apostolic revelation with contemporary claims of private visions.

context: Paul, he says that he had these visions and he had these revelations, but he says that he cannot, it's not lawful for a man to utter
point_summary: Paul's inability to utter what he heard in paradise is used to challenge modern claims of heavenly visions and new revelations, arguing the closed canon renders such accounts unnecessary and suspect.

context: Notice what the Bible says in verse number six. For though I would desire to glory, I shall not be a fool, for I will say the truth. But now I forbear, lest any man should think of me above that which he seemeth me to be, or that he heareth of me. And lest I should be exalted above measure, through the abundance of the revelation, there was given to me a thorn in the flesh.
point_summary: Paul's restraint from boasting in verse 6 sets the stage for understanding why God gave him a thorn—to guard against the pride that extraordinary revelation could produce.

context: There was given to me a thorn in the flesh
point_summary: God sovereignly gave Paul the thorn — just as He gave the visions — to prevent pride from destroying him, making the thorn not a curse but a necessary, God-ordained reason to rejoice.

context: lest I should be exalted above measure
point_summary: God's sovereign gift of the thorn — given by the same hand that gave the visions — was a necessary, rejoice-worthy guard against the pride that would have destroyed Paul.

context: there was given to me a thorn in the flesh. The messenger of Satan to buffet me lest I should be exalted above measure.
point_summary: God's deliberate gift of the thorn — bracketed by the repeated phrase "lest I should be exalted above measure" — reveals that He ordains affliction specifically to guard His people against the pride He resists.

context: He gave him a thorn, this experience of these visions and revelations could lead to pride, could lead to glory, but we find God in His infinite wisdom, He gave to Paul this thorn in the flesh
point_summary: The thorn in the flesh is framed as a divine instrument of humility, with the repeated phrase "lest I should be exalted above measure" bookending verse 7 to show God's deliberate purpose in keeping Paul from pride.

context: Paul besought the Lord that this thing would depart
point_summary: Paul's threefold plea for the thorn's removal illustrates that God sometimes answers persistent prayer with a sovereign "no" because He knows what circumstances are necessary to keep His servants humble.

context: lest I should be exalted above measure
point_summary: God uses the thorn given to Paul — framed by the repeated phrase "lest I should be exalted above measure" — to produce both humility and dependence upon Him in the believer's life.

context: for this thing, I besought the Lord thrice that it might depart from me
point_summary: God's power to remove any burden is the very reason Paul prayed three times for the thorn's removal — earnest, repeated petition rooted in confidence in an omnipotent God.

context: Paul besought the Lord thrice
point_summary: Paul's threefold plea for the thorn's removal is presented as earnest, faith-driven prayer—yet God's answer, though different than Paul desired, is shown to be better than what was asked.

context: I have assaulted the Lord thrice, that it might depart from me
point_summary: Even when God answers prayer differently than requested, his refusal to remove the thorn reveals a greater principle: his grace is sufficient, and his strength is perfected precisely in weakness.

context: The Lord said unto me, my grace is sufficient for thee. For my strength is made perfect in weakness
point_summary: God's refusal to remove Paul's thorn was not a lesser answer but a greater one, revealing the sufficiency of grace as a principle more valuable than the relief Paul sought.

context: for my strength is made perfect in weakness
point_summary: God's declaration that "my grace is sufficient" is present tense, signifying that His enabling grace meets every believer in whatever moment they face, not merely in past salvation or future promise.

context: God's grace is sufficient for you and it is sufficient for me
point_summary: God's enabling grace — distinct from saving grace — is present-tense and sufficient in the actual moment of need, not hypothetically in advance, meaning it will not run out whenever that moment arrives.

context: the God of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious Gospel should shine unto them
point_summary: Satan's blinding of unbelievers' minds (2 Cor. 4:4) is cited to check doctrinal pride: the lost are spiritually blind, making believers responsible to reach them humbly, not superior to look down on them.

context: He was given a thorn, and he was given an answer
point_summary: God's gift of doctrinal truth carries responsibility rather than superiority, and the thorn given to Paul illustrates that even extraordinary revelation demands the counterweight of humility.

context: we are his ambassadors
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: If any man be in Christ, he's a new creature
point_summary: Regeneration through the gospel is not merely doctrinal assertion but personal testimony — the preacher cites 2 Corinthians 5:17 as confirmation that salvation genuinely transforms the believer into a new creature.

context: today is the day of salvation
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we studied through the book of 2 Corinthians and we finished up with a final message just last Sunday morning
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: today is the day of salvation
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: Christ's substitutionary death—he who knew no sin was made sin for us—is applied personally to his own unbelieving siblings, including James, as those for whom he died.

context: the little G God of this world hath blinded the minds of them that believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel should shine unto them
point_summary: Satan's blinding of unbelieving minds (2 Cor. 4:4) explains the opposition gospel preachers face, yet that same gospel retains the power of God unto salvation.

context: Corinthians chapter number 12, 13 this morning. Chapter number 13
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Chapter number 13, almost to the end of the book
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Back to 2nd Corinthians, of course we understand over the last couple of weeks as we've studied
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: in chapter 12 and verse number 11, he says, I am become a fool in glorying, ye have compelled me
point_summary: Paul's self-defense in chapters 11–12, culminating in 12:11, is framed as a reluctant but necessary response to false teachers whose criticisms compelled him to commend himself rather than be commended by the Corinthians.

context: this is the third time I am coming to you. In the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established
point_summary: Paul's citation of the two-or-three-witnesses standard frames his third visit as a formal, decisive moment of accountability, bringing the Corinthians to a point where a response—or non-response—constitutes a verdict.

context: I told you before and foretell you as if I were present the second time and being absent now, I write to them which heretofore have sinned
point_summary: Paul's warning in verse 2 that he will "not spare" when he comes again illustrates that refusing to respond to truth is itself a decision, making inaction a form of accountability.

context: Since you seek a proof of Christ speaking in me, which to you word is not weak, but is mighty in you
point_summary: Paul's apostolic authority is validated not by his own assertion but by Christ's powerful working among the Corinthians themselves, making their experience the proof they demanded.

context: For though he was crucified through weakness, yet he liveth by the power of God. For we also are weak in him, but we shall live with him by the power of God towards you
point_summary: Christ's crucifixion in weakness and resurrection by God's power form the pattern Paul applies to himself and the Corinthians as the basis for his apostolic authority among them.

context: examine yourselves, whether you be in the faith, prove your own selves, know ye not your own selves
point_summary: Paul's command to "examine yourselves" in verse 5 serves as the sermon's governing text and the decisive moment to which the entire epistle's study has been building, demanding a response from hearers.

context: examine yourselves, examine yourselves
point_summary: Self-examination of one's standing "in the faith" is the sermon's governing imperative, with verse 5's command to "examine yourselves" providing both the title and the central challenge Paul presses upon the Corinthian believers.

reference_text: this is the third time I am coming to you
context: this is the third time I am coming to you
point_summary: Paul's citation of the Deuteronomy 17 witness principle within a New Testament epistle serves as evidence that Old Testament law retains practical benefit and applicable authority for believers today.

reference_text: in the mouth of two or three witnesses, shall every word be established
context: in the mouth of two or three witnesses, shall every word be established
point_summary: Paul's citation of the Deuteronomy 17 witness principle within a New Testament epistle demonstrates that Old Testament law retains practical benefit and applicable authority for believers today.

context: he tells them in verse one, this is the third time
point_summary: Paul's citation of the Old Testament "two or three witnesses" standard demonstrates that Old Testament legal principles retain binding applicability for New Testament believers.

context: in verse number two, he says, I told you before and foretell you as if I was present
point_summary: Paul's repeated warning — unchanged whether spoken in person or written from a distance — demonstrates that apostolic truth does not shift with time, circumstance, or the audience's perception of the messenger's weakness.

context: he's absent and he's writing to them in verse number two
point_summary: Paul's repeated warning — unchanged whether spoken in person or written from a distance — demonstrates that apostolic truth does not shift with time, circumstance, or the audience's perception of the messenger's weakness.

context: since ye seek a proof of Christ speaking in me, which to you word is not weak, but is mighty in you
point_summary: Paul's demand for proof of Christ speaking through him anchors the sermon's point that God's word is settled and unchanging, consistent across Paul's ministry regardless of the Corinthians' doubts.

context: For though he was crucified through weakness, yet he liveth by the power of God
point_summary: Christ's crucifixion, though accomplished in weakness, is inseparable from His resurrection power — a pairing Paul invokes to ground the authority of his apostolic message to the Corinthians.

context: my grace is sufficient for thee, for my strength is made perfect in what? In weakness
point_summary: God's strength being made perfect in weakness (2 Cor. 12:9) is invoked to explain why Paul embraces weakness rather than resisting it — because dependence on God follows from it, and pride forfeits His grace.

context: the apostle Paul besought the Lord thrice that this thorn would depart from him
point_summary: God's strength being made perfect in weakness, promised to Paul in response to his threefold prayer over the thorn, is the foundation Paul draws on when telling the Corinthians that apostolic weakness is the very condition through which divine power operates.

context: examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith
point_summary: Paul's command to "examine yourselves" in verse 5 is framed not as a suggestion but as a direct apostolic demand upon the Corinthian believers to verify whether Christ is truly in them.

context: prove your own selves. Know ye not, notice again, your own selves, that Jesus Christ is in you, except ye be reprobates
point_summary: Paul's command "examine yourselves" in verse 5 is framed as a divine demand, redirecting the Corinthians' critical scrutiny of Paul inward upon their own standing in the faith.

context: But I trust, verse number six, that ye shall know that we are not reprobates
point_summary: Paul's confidence that the Corinthians will recognize his apostolic integrity reframes their scrutiny of him: they had been examining Paul when the real demand was to examine themselves.

context: for we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ
point_summary: The individual accountability of every believer before the judgment seat of Christ grounds the call to self-examination, since no one else will stand in your place before God.

context: examine yourselves whether you be in the faith. Prove your own selves
point_summary: Self-examination commanded in this verse is intensely personal — church attendance, sound preaching, and right standards save no one; each individual must know whether Christ is truly in them.

reference_text: Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith
context: Examine yourselves, whether you be in the faith
point_summary: Self-examination precedes and disarms external scrutiny: because Paul habitually examined his own conscience before God and men, others' judgment of him carried little weight.

context: but I trust that you shall know that we are not reprobates
point_summary: Paul's confidence under scrutiny rested on a self-examined conscience, so that when others tested him, he trusted they would find he was no reprobate but had passed the test.

context: now I pray to God that ye do no evil
point_summary: Paul's prayer that the Corinthians "do no evil" reflects his pastoral concern not for his own vindication but for their righteous conduct and worthy walk.

context: verses 20 and 21 of chapter 12 about the dealing with sin
point_summary: Paul's earlier catalog of sins in chapter 12 (vv. 20–21) is cited as the basis for his prayer in 13:7 that the Corinthians would do no evil, linking the concern over unaddressed sin to his pastoral desire for their righteous conduct.

context: verse number two about those that have sinned
point_summary: Paul's earlier warning to those who had sinned (v. 2) is recalled here as the basis for his present prayer that the Corinthians would do no evil.

context: that which cometh upon me daily, the care of all the churches
point_summary: Paul's daily burden for "the care of all the churches" (2 Cor. 11:28) is cited as the motivating concern behind his desire for the Corinthians' spiritual maturity, knowing they will one day stand before God.

context: we read that in 2 Corinthians chapter number one
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I preached this several months ago from the book of 2 Corinthians
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: therefore I write these things being absent, lest being present I should use sharpness according to the power which the Lord hath given me to edification and not to destruction
point_summary: Paul's written appeal to Corinth reflects his preference that apostolic authority be used for edification rather than sharpness, hoping the letter itself would prompt repentance before his arrival.

context: therefore I write these things being absent, lest being present I should use sharpness
point_summary: Paul's authority to act with sharpness upon arrival was given for building up, not tearing down, and his written letter was intended to move the Corinthians to repentance before that authority needed to be exercised in person.

context: for we can do nothing against the truth, but for the truth
point_summary: Paul's inability to act against the truth means that those who have embraced and applied God's Word need not fear examination, because the truth itself vindicates faithful obedience.

context: examine ourselves
point_summary: Self-examination is not a fearful audit for the believer who walks uprightly, but a regular, honest accounting of spiritual growth and obedience to God's Word.

context: You're going to stand before God one day
point_summary: Those who live with a conscience void of offense before God need not fear the coming judgment, because faithful daily obedience prepares one to give a good account before the Lord.

context: our God is the God of all comfort, and He comforteth us in all of our tribulations
point_summary: God is identified as the ultimate source of all comfort, yet the sermon illustrates that He channels that comfort through people—as He did for Paul through the coming of Titus.

context: Paul was comforted by the coming of Titus
point_summary: God himself is the source of all comfort, but he channels that comfort through people—illustrated by how he used Titus to bring comfort to Paul, the very man who wrote that God "comforteth us in all our tribulations."

context: Therefore, if any man be in Christ, he's a new creature. Old things are passed away. Behold, all things have become new.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: old things are passed away. Behold, all things have become new
point_summary: Being a new creature in Christ (2 Cor. 5:17) means the believer now experiences an internal conflict between the Spirit and the flesh, rather than living solely to fulfill fleshly lusts as before salvation.

context: as we looked in chapter number four, that there will be pressures in ministry
point_summary: Ministry in 2 Corinthians 4 brings both internal and external pressures, yet God's provision proves sufficient to sustain those called to that work.

context: As we enter into chapter number five, we find that the ministry that we've been given according to chapter four is defined for us in chapter five as being the ministry of reconciliation
point_summary: Chapter 5 defines the ministry introduced in chapter 4 as the ministry of reconciliation, in which believers serve as Christ's ambassadors calling sinners at enmity with God to be reconciled to Him.

context: we pray you in Christ's stead, be reconciled to God. That's what verse 20 says in 2 Corinthians chapter five
point_summary: Believers are identified as ambassadors entrusted with the ministry of reconciliation, pleading on Christ's behalf for sinners at enmity with God to be reconciled to Him.

context: we then, and then notice this phrase, as workers together with him, beseech you also that ye receive not the grace of God in vain
point_summary: The phrase "workers together with him" in 2 Corinthians 6:1 identifies the believer's co-labor with God as the greatest and most defining aspect of the ministry of reconciliation entrusted to the church.

context: we are workers together with God
point_summary: The declaration that believers are "workers together with God" in 2 Corinthians 6:1 is not a new truth, but one Paul had already grounded in 1 Corinthians 3, where laborers are nothing apart from God who gives the increase.

context: as workers together with him
point_summary: The plural "we" of 2 Corinthians 6:1 signals that gospel ministry is a cooperative partnership—not individual achievement—in which believers labor together with one another and with God himself.

context: we then as workers together with him beseech you also that ye receive not the grace of God in vain
point_summary: Working together with God in the ministry of reconciliation is the greatest work on earth, and Paul's warning against receiving God's grace in vain applies both to saving grace and to the serving grace that enables ministry.

context: we beseech you that ye receive not the grace of God in vain
point_summary: Working together with Christ in ministry means that the grace enabling believers to serve is not merely saving grace but also a "serving grace" that must not be received in vain.

context: now is the day of salvation
point_summary: Paul's quotation of Isaiah 49 in 2 Corinthians 6:2 establishes the urgency of ministry: the sinner does not know when he will draw his final breath and must not delay reconciliation.

context: as ambassadors were the ones that have been already reconciled to God
point_summary: The urgency of the gospel appeal belongs not only to the lost sinner but primarily to the reconciled ambassador, who has been entrusted with the message and must deliver it without delay.

context: we find that there's an urgency on the part of the ambassadors to deliver the message why because we know the truth we've been entrusted with the truth and there's an urgency about it we find every day you read about people that that have entered into eternity
point_summary: Those already reconciled to God through Christ (v. 18) are thereby entrusted with the ministry of reconciliation, making the urgency to deliver that message fall primarily on the ambassador, not the lost sinner.

context: giving no offense in anything that the ministry be not blamed
point_summary: Paul's consuming concern in verse 3 is that a minister's personal conduct must never become the obstacle that causes someone to reject the gospel message itself.

context: what concord hath Christ with Belial light with darkness
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: but in all things approving ourselves as the ministers of God in much patience, in afflictions, in necessities, in distresses, in stripes, in imprisonments, in tumults, in labors, in watchings, in fastings
point_summary: Faithful ministry is authenticated not by comfort but by endurance through a catalogued list of sufferings—afflictions, necessities, distresses, stripes, imprisonments, and tumults—which both Paul and all who serve God can expect to face.

context: pureness and knowledge and long-suffering and kindness
point_summary: The qualities listed in verse 6 — pureness, knowledge, longsuffering, kindness — are endured not in human strength but specifically through the Holy Ghost, without whom such conduct is impossible.

context: by the Holy Ghost
point_summary: Enduring ministry is sustained not by human strength but by the Holy Spirit's continuous filling — the question being not how much of the Spirit one possesses, but how much the Spirit possesses of the believer.

context: by the Word of Truth
point_summary: Endurance in ministry is sustained not only by the Holy Spirit but by the Word of Truth, which produces settled conviction and forward resolve regardless of circumstances.

context: by the Holy Ghost, by the word of truth, by the power of God, by the armor of righteousness
point_summary: Endurance in ministry comes not through human strength but through divine resources—the Word of truth, the power of God, and the armor of righteousness—because the Christian life is a battleground, not a playground.

context: as we looked at chapter 2 a number of months ago
point_summary: The Christian life is a battleground requiring the full armor of God, and Paul's endurance through it depended on the Holy Spirit, the word of truth, God's power, and the armor of righteousness.

context: In verses 8 through 10, we find that the apostle Paul begins to share some things
point_summary: The Christian life is a battleground, not a playground, and the minister endures its hardships—including misunderstanding, dishonor, and evil report—through the Holy Ghost, the word of truth, and the armor of righteousness.

context: as dying and behold, we live as chastened and not killed. As sorrowful yet always rejoicing. As poor yet making many rich. As having nothing and yet possessing all things
point_summary: Faithful ministry endures the paradox of being dismissed as unknown by men while remaining fully known and accountable to God, making human recognition irrelevant to the servant's calling.

context: For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ that though he was rich yet for your sakes he became poor. That ye through his poverty might be rich
point_summary: Christ's voluntary self-impoverishment, becoming poor though rich so believers might be made rich, grounds the contentment and perspective Paul models amid hardship and human opposition.

context: we are workers together with God
point_summary: Being "workers together with God" carries both the privilege of divine partnership and an urgent call to resolve that holds regardless of cost or persecution.

context: 2 Corinthians chapter number 12
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 2 Corinthians chapter 13
point_summary: Paul's love for the Corinthians in 2 Corinthians 12–13 is presented as a further development of the charity teaching he had already given this same congregation in 1 Corinthians 13.

context: beginning in verse number 11 of 2 Corinthians chapter 12, notice the Bible says this, I am become a fool in glorying
point_summary: Paul's self-deprecating claim to be "a fool in glorying" reflects the compulsion the Corinthians placed on him to defend his apostolic credentials against false teachers rather than his own desire for self-promotion.

context: he started this back at the beginning of chapter number 11
point_summary: Paul's self-defense in chapter 11—distinguishing himself from false teachers to prove his apostleship—is presented as the backdrop that makes his love for the Corinthians, not self-promotion, the driving motive of the letter.

context: I am become a fool in glorying, ye have compelled me. Then notice this, for I ought to have been commended of you. For in nothing am I behind the very chiefest apostles, though I be nothing.
point_summary: Paul's reluctant self-defense before the Corinthians—boasting he calls foolish—was forced upon him by their failure to commend him against false apostles who questioned his authority.

context: Truly the signs of an apostle were wrought among you in all patience and signs and wonders and mighty deeds.
point_summary: Signs and wonders authenticated Paul's apostleship among the Corinthians, serving as evidence he cites to distinguish himself from the false teachers who had compelled this defense.

context: For what is it wherein ye were inferior to other churches, except it be that I myself was not burdensome to you?
point_summary: Paul's refusal to be financially burdensome to the Corinthians is presented as a concrete expression of his love for them, not a wrong requiring forgiveness.

context: Verse 14, behold, the third time I am ready to come to you, and I will not be burdensome to you, for I seek not yours, but you. For the children ought not to lay up for the parents, but the parents for the children.
point_summary: Paul's refusal to be financially burdensome to the Corinthians a third time illustrates his pastoral principle of seeking the people themselves, not their possessions.

context: And I will very gladly spend and be spent for you, though the more abundantly I love you, the less I be loved.
point_summary: Parental love that spends itself entirely for its children, even when that love goes unreciprocated, illustrates Paul's selfless commitment to the Corinthians' spiritual good.

context: But be it so, I did not burden you. Nevertheless, being crafty, I caught you with Guile. Did I make a gain of you by any of them whom I sent unto you?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: Walked we not in the same spirit? Walked we not in the same steps?
context: Walked we not in the same spirit? Walked we not in the same steps?
point_summary: Titus's conduct among the Corinthians—walking in the same spirit and steps as Paul—serves as evidence refuting the charge that Paul exploited them through those he sent.

reference_text: think ye that we excuse ourselves unto you? We speak before God in Christ, but we do all things dearly beloved for your edifying
context: think ye that we excuse ourselves unto you? We speak before God in Christ, but we do all things dearly beloved for your edifying
point_summary: Paul's defense before the Corinthians was not self-justification but was spoken before God in Christ, with their edification as its sole aim.

context: Paul points out right in the beginning in verse number 11. He says, for I ought to have been commended of you.
point_summary: Despite being criticized and questioned by the very people he served, Paul insists the Corinthians themselves should have been his defenders, since his apostolic labor among them was self-evident.

context: at the end of verse 16, he's rehearsing and recounting what they would say
point_summary: Paul's rehearsal of the accusation in verse 16 — that he was crafty and exploiting the Corinthians through the offering — frames the charges he then refutes throughout the passage.

context: I will very gladly spend and be spent for you
point_summary: Paul's self-described conduct—gladly spending and being spent for the Corinthians—is held up as evidence that their failure to defend him was unwarranted, since his sacrificial investment in them should have earned their commendation.

context: I am become a fool in glory, ye have compelled me, for I ought to have been commended of you. For in nothing am I behind the very chiefest apostles, though I be nothing
point_summary: Paul's self-description as "nothing" alongside his refusal to yield ground to the "super apostles" reflects a proper humility that neither diminishes his God-given apostolic calling nor concedes authority to false teachers.

context: I in nothing am I behind the very chiefest apostles
point_summary: Paul's claim to be "nothing" while simultaneously asserting he is behind none of the chiefest apostles reveals a proper self-view: apostolic authority rested in God's calling, not personal merit.

context: truly the signs of an apostle were wrought among you in all patience, in signs and wonders and mighty deeds
point_summary: Paul's apostolic credentials among the Corinthians were demonstrated not by self-promotion but by the patient working of signs, wonders, and mighty deeds in their midst.

context: and that which cometh upon me daily, the care of all the churches
point_summary: Paul's daily burden for all the churches (2 Cor. 11:28) is cited as evidence that his love extended beyond Philippi to every congregation he served, including Corinth.

context: in verse 12, back in 2 Corinthians 12, he mentions about the things that were done there in the ministry
point_summary: Apostolic ministry is evidenced not by financial claims but by the time and relational investment poured into a congregation, as Paul's 18 months in Corinth demonstrate.

context: as we continue in verse 13
point_summary: Paul's refusal to accept financial support from the Corinthians—though he had every right to receive it—was the one way they differed from other churches, a distinction he frames as a self-imposed burden borne out of love.

context: for that is it wherein you were inferior to other churches, except it be that I myself was not burdensome to you
point_summary: Paul's refusal to accept financial support from Corinth, while legitimate, deprived the church of fulfilling its biblical responsibility to compensate its minister—a deprivation he ironically calls a "wrong" done to them.

context: that's what Paul's alluding to there in verse 13
point_summary: Paul's deliberate refusal to accept financial support from Corinth, though it was their rightful biblical responsibility, was the one area in which he acknowledged shortchanging the church.

context: 'Behold, the third time I am ready to come,' verse 14, 'Behold, the third time I am ready 'to come to you and I will not be burdensome to you.'
point_summary: Paul's refusal to seek financial support from the Corinthians demonstrated that his love was for the people themselves, not their possessions — "I seek not yours, but you."

context: 'For I seek not yours, but you.'
point_summary: Paul's love for the Corinthians is demonstrated by his deliberate refusal to be financially burdensome, seeking the people themselves rather than their material resources.

context: That I seek not yours, but you
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I seek not yours, but you" is presented as the defining evidence that his repeated willingness to come to Corinth without financial burden was rooted in genuine love, not personal gain.

context: in verse 16, down through verse number 18
point_summary: Paul's refusal to be a financial burden, though slandered as crafty manipulation by his opponents, is defended through the consistent, selfless actions of himself and his co-workers as proof of genuine love.

context: Walked we not in the same spirit? Walked we not in the same steps?
point_summary: Paul's defense against accusations of financial cunning rests on the consistent conduct of Titus and his companion, whose identical spirit and steps confirmed that no one exploited the Corinthians for personal gain.

context: I will very gladly spend and be spent for you
point_summary: Paul's unrequited love for the Corinthians — loving them more abundantly even as they loved him less — is presented as love motivated by obedience to Christ rather than any expectation of return.

context: Though the more abundantly I love you, the less I be loved
point_summary: Paul's love for the Corinthians was not transactional—he loved them more abundantly even as they loved him less, motivated by obedience to Christ rather than any expectation of return.

context: I will very gladly spend and be spent for you
point_summary: Paul's willingness to spend and be spent for the Corinthians—even while receiving less love in return—is presented as the defining action that proves the sincerity of his declared love for them.

context: think ye that we excuse ourselves unto you
point_summary: Paul's stated purpose — "we do all things, dearly beloved, for your edifying" — is presented as the clearest evidence of his genuine love for the Corinthians, contrasting his servant-hearted motive with the Judaizers' self-serving use of authority.

context: for though I should boast somewhat more of our authority, which the Lord hath given us for edification and not for your destruction, I should not be ashamed
point_summary: Apostolic authority, Paul insists, was granted by the Lord not for domination or personal gain but strictly for the edification of believers—a purpose that distinguished him from those who exploited their power.

context: In the book of 2 Corinthians, these are people that Paul's writing to
point_summary: Paul's authority as an apostle was given not for personal gain or control but solely for the edification and building up of believers, distinguishing true ministry from the self-serving manipulation of the Judaizers.

context: 2 Corinthians chapter number 12
point_summary: Paul's unrequited love for the Corinthians—loving them more abundantly while receiving less love in return—stands in contrast to the model brotherly love commended in the Thessalonian church.

context: the more abundantly I love you, the less I be loved
point_summary: Paul's unrequited love for the Corinthians—loving them more abundantly while receiving less love in return—is presented as evidence that Spirit-empowered love persists regardless of the response it receives.

context: Paul, his love for the Corinthians is evidenced there in that text of Scripture in 2 Corinthians chapter 12
point_summary: Paul's self-giving love for the Corinthians—spending and being spent for them—stands as a human, flesh-and-blood example of the love by which disciples are known.

context: I'll gladly spend and be spent for you
point_summary: Paul's willingness to "gladly spend and be spent" for the Corinthians is held up as a concrete human example of sacrificial love that believers, enabled by the Holy Spirit, are called to imitate.

context: if any man be in Christ, he's a new creature. Hope things are passed away behold all things have become new
point_summary: Regeneration in Christ produces a visible difference in behavior that others will notice, just as Rahab's scarlet cord was a public testimony of her changed allegiance.

context: we're to be a cheerful giver
point_summary: Giving an offering is presented as an act of faith, trusting that God will make 90% go further than 100% — faith evidenced by the tangible act of placing something in the plate.

context: if we give sparingly, we're gonna reap sparingly. We give bountifully, we'll reap bountifully
point_summary: Giving a tithe is presented as a concrete act of faith, trusting that God will make 90% go further than 100% — the sowing-and-reaping principle applied to financial obedience.

context: God hath made Him to be sin who knew no sin that we might be made to righteousness of God in Him
point_summary: God's incomparable holiness raises the question of how sinful man can approach Him, answered in Christ's substitutionary exchange — made sin so that we might become the righteousness of God.

context: the judgment seat of Christ in 2 Corinthians chapter 5 and verse 10
point_summary: Even believers will stand before the judgment seat of Christ, and the terror that follows that reality is what compels urgent, fear-driven evangelism.

context: it says in verse number 11, knowing therefore the terror of the Lord, we persuade men, but we are made manifest unto God
point_summary: The terror of the Lord named in 2 Corinthians 5:11 is presented as the motivating force behind evangelism, set within the broader call for God's people to recover a genuine fear of God.

context: he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: Gratitude that God's wrath fell on Christ at Calvary—making believers the righteousness of God—grounds rather than removes the healthy fear of God that every believer should maintain.

context: Turn over just for a moment, if you will, to the book of 2 Corinthians in the fourth chapter
point_summary: Spiritual victory over the enemy comes not through physical strength but through the indwelling Word, as Paul's own body-worn-yet-inwardly-strengthened life illustrates in 2 Corinthians 4.

reference_text: 2 Corinthians chapter number four and verse number one
context: Notice in 2 Corinthians chapter number four and verse number one, therefore, seeing we have this ministry as we have received mercy, notice this phrase, we faint not.
point_summary: The phrase "we faint not" in 2 Corinthians 4:1 is cited as evidence that the word of God abiding in the believer sustains inner strength through outward trial and adversity.

context: we have this treasure in earth and vessels that the excellency of the power may be of God and not of us
point_summary: The treasure of God's power dwelling in frail, earthen vessels is what enables believers to face relentless trials without fainting, sustained by the inward renewal that comes through the Word.

context: We are troubled on every side yet not distressed. We are perplexed but not in despair, persecuted but not forsaken, cast down but not destroyed
point_summary: Spiritual endurance under trial — "we faint not" — is grounded not in outward circumstances but in the daily inward renewal that comes through abiding in the Word of God.

context: always bearing about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body
point_summary: Bearing in the body the dying of Jesus so that His life is made manifest is the apostolic pattern by which believers do not faint amid trials, sustained by the inward renewal the Word of God provides.

context: But though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed day by day
point_summary: Spiritual resilience against adversity is grounded in the daily renewal of the inward man through the Word of God, which is the reason believers do not faint.

context: we're not ignorant of his devices
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: behold, today is the day of salvation
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: books in my mind, 2 Corinthians and the book of James
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: God's grace is sufficient, as we studied all year last year, studying through the book of 2 Corinthians, and rejoicing in that wonderful truth and promise that God gave to us in 2 Corinthians chapter 12 in response to Paul's prayer
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: look back, if you will, at 2 Corinthians, I want to remind you of some of the truth that we looked at in chapter 13 of the book of 2 Corinthians
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for we are glad when we are weak, and ye are strong. In this also we wish even your perfection
point_summary: Paul's prayer for the Corinthians' "perfection" in 2 Corinthians 13:9 is cited as a parallel to James 1:4, with both passages using "perfect" to mean spiritual maturity.

context: our God, who is the God of all comfort, comforteth me in all of my difficulties
point_summary: God's comfort in trials is purposeful—received so that it may be passed on to others in their difficulties.

context: Would to God ye could bear with me a little in my folly, and indeed bear with me
point_summary: Paul's reluctant appeal for the Corinthians to "bear with him a little in his folly" is framed as sarcastic self-defense, forced upon him by false teachers demanding proof of his apostolic credentials.

context: verses 16 down through verse number 33 here in 2 Corinthians chapter number 11
point_summary: Paul's reluctant entry into "boasting" is framed as sarcastic self-defense forced upon him by false teachers who demanded apostolic credentials from him.

context: the first 15 verses of 2 Corinthians chapter number 11
point_summary: Paul's appeal for the Corinthians to "bear with him in his folly" is framed as reluctant self-defense, forced upon him by false teachers demanding proof of his apostolic credentials.

context: Bear with me a little in my folly
point_summary: Paul's reluctant appeal to "bear with me a little in my folly" is framed as sarcasm forced upon him by the Corinthian church's tolerance of boastful false teachers demanding apostolic credentials.

context: he says in verse number 16, if otherwise yet as a fool receive me that I may boast myself a little
point_summary: Paul's reluctant self-commendation in verse 16 is framed as a concession to Corinth's climate, where false teachers forced him to descend to their level of boasting to be heard.

context: And in this confidence boasting, verse 18
point_summary: Paul's willingness to "glory after the flesh" is framed not as genuine self-promotion but as a concession to Corinth's climate, where false teachers had made fleshly boasting the currency of credibility.

context: this passage of Scripture in 2 Corinthians 11
point_summary: Suffering precedes resurrection glory, and Paul's catalog of trials in 2 Corinthians 11 illustrates what "the fellowship of his sufferings" looks like in the life of a faithful minister.

context: Are they Hebrews? So am I. Are they Israelites? So am I. Are they the seed of Abraham? So am I. Are they ministers of Christ? I speak as a fool. I am more.
point_summary: Paul's apostolic credentials — Hebrew, Israelite, seed of Abraham — are introduced here not as the sermon's focus but as the launching point for cataloguing the trials that authenticate his ministry.

context: in labors more abundant, in stripes above measure, in prisons more frequent, in deaths oft
point_summary: Paul's catalog of sufferings in verse 23 — labors, stripes, imprisonments, and frequent brushes with death — establishes that trials are not exceptional but expected realities of the Christian life.

context: beginning in verse 23, I read it already, where he says in stripes, in labors more abundantly, in stripes above measure, in prisons more frequent, in deaths often
point_summary: Paul's catalog of external sufferings beginning in verse 23 — stripes, prisons, and deaths — is presented as the apostle explaining to the Corinthians that trials are not only to be expected but take concrete, identifiable forms in the believer's life.

context: Paul begins in verse 23 and really down through verse number 27 dealing with external trials
point_summary: Paul's catalog of external sufferings in verses 23–27 is presented as evidence that trials are not anomalies for believers but expected, purposeful realities that God permits and works through.

context: He says in verse 24, of the Jews, five times received I 40 stripes save one. 39.
point_summary: Paul's five receptions of thirty-nine lashes illustrate the calculated, near-lethal brutality of external trials endured in apostolic ministry—suffering inflicted to the very threshold of death.

context: We find that in verse number 25, thrice was I beaten with rods. Once was I stoned. Thrice I suffered shipwreck. A night and a day I've been in the deep.
point_summary: Paul's catalog of external trials in verse 25 — beatings, stoning, shipwrecks — illustrates that suffering comes from multiple fronts as part of the apostle's explained, expected experience.

context: In journeyings often, in perils of waters, in perils of robbers, in perils by my own countrymen, in perils by the heathen, in perils in the city, in perils in the wilderness, in perils in the sea, in perils among false brethren.
point_summary: Verse 26 appears within Paul's catalog of external trials, illustrating that danger surrounded the apostle from every direction—waters, robbers, countrymen, heathen, city, wilderness, sea, and false brethren alike.

context: In weariness and painfulness, in watchings often
point_summary: Paul's catalog of physical hardships in verse 27—hunger, thirst, cold, nakedness—illustrates the category of external trials believers are called to expect and endure.

context: in hunger and thirst, in fastings often, in cold and nakedness
point_summary: Paul's catalog of sufferings in verses 25–27 — beatings, shipwreck, hunger, cold, nakedness — is presented as the direct cost of faithful gospel preaching, not the consequence of wrongdoing.

context: Beside those things that are without, that which cometh upon me daily. Notice what he says, the care of all the churches.
point_summary: Beyond physical persecution, Paul's deepest daily burden was internal — the pastoral weight of caring for all the churches, feeling their weakness and offense as his own.

context: who is weak and I am not weak, who is offended and I burn not
point_summary: Paul's deepest internal trial was not physical suffering but the pastoral burden of feeling every weakness and offense experienced by the churches he loved.

context: that which cometh upon me daily, the care of all the churches
point_summary: Paul's daily burden for all the churches he planted was a pastoral weight heavier even than physical persecution—a constant, parental-like anxiety over whether his spiritual children were walking in truth and resisting false teachers.

context: 2 Corinthians 11. I've never come close to suffering 1 10th of 1% of what Paul details in 2 Corinthians chapter 11.
point_summary: Paul's catalog of sufferings in 2 Corinthians 11 sets a standard most Western believers have never approached, yet trials remain universal and expected for those who follow Christ.

context: if I must needs glory, I will glory of the things which concern mine infirmities
point_summary: Paul's boasting only in his infirmities, rather than in achievements, ensures that any glory arising from his sufferings points to the Lord rather than to himself.

context: Paul says in verse 31, the God and father of our Lord Jesus Christ which is blessed forevermore knoweth that I lie not
point_summary: Paul's oath-like appeal to God as witness — "the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ…knoweth that I lie not" — grounds his boasting in infirmities with divine verification rather than self-promotion.

context: if I'm going to glory, I'm gonna glory in the things concerning mine infirmities
point_summary: Boasting in weakness rather than strength is validated by Paul's own testimony of escaping Damascus in a basket—his first post-conversion crisis—as the defining example of glory through infirmity.

context: If any man be in Christ, he's a new creature. Old things are passed away, behold, all things are become new.
point_summary: Paul's conversion on the Damascus road—from persecutor to preacher—is cited as the living proof that in Christ a man becomes a new creature, with old things literally passed away.

context: and through a window and a basket was I let down by the wall and escaped His hands
point_summary: Paul's deliberate choice to close his catalog of sufferings with the basket-escape at Damascus—his very first deliverance—establishes that God's proven faithfulness from the beginning was the foundation for enduring every subsequent trial.

context: I'm going to glory about those things concerning my infirmities
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: that wonderful promise that we read in 2 Corinthians chapter 12
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: in 2 Corinthians chapter number eight and verse number 16
point_summary: God's concern for the undivided heart is grounded in His own initiative, as seen in Paul's observation that God placed earnest care for others within Titus's heart.

context: I'm going to preach a message next week on Sunday morning from 2 Corinthians 8
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Second Corinthians chapter number 10 this morning
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I preached a message from Second Corinthians 12 to begin the year
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we've been walking through the book of Second Corinthians on Sunday mornings. And here we find ourselves in chapter number 10 this morning
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Second Corinthians chapter 10 verses one through 18 this morning
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for not he that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord commendeth
point_summary: Self-commendation carries no weight before God; only the Lord's commendation constitutes true approval, and this verse frames Paul's entire refusal to compete with the Judaizers on their own self-promoting terms.

context: wherefore we labor that whether present or absent, we may be accepted of him
point_summary: Paul's singular ambition — laboring to be accepted by God whether present or absent — establishes the controlling motive that unifies his defense of ministry throughout 2 Corinthians: divine approval, not self-commendation.

context: that he died for all, speaking about the Lord Jesus Christ, God in flesh, that he died for all, why? Because all are sinners in need of a savior. They have a sin debt that can only be paid for with the blood of Christ. And so he died for all that they would live. Notice this should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him, which died for them and rose again
point_summary: Christ's atoning death obligates believers to live not for themselves but for him, grounding Paul's entire ministry ambition in seeking God's approval rather than self-commendation.

context: in second Corinthians five, just like he's going to here in second Corinthians chapter number 10
point_summary: Paul's labor to be "accepted of him" in 2 Corinthians 5:9, read alongside verse 15's call to live unto Christ rather than self, establishes the same desire for God's approval that drives the argument of 2 Corinthians 10.

context: here in second Corinthians chapter number 10
point_summary: Paul's desire for God's approval in 2 Corinthians 10 parallels his earlier statement in 5:9 — both passages reflect a longing for divine acceptance that is distinct from, not a condition of, salvation.

context: as we look through 2 Corinthians chapter number 10, if you're taking notes, notice with me, first of all, Paul's approach. In verses one through six
point_summary: Paul's approach to ministry in 2 Corinthians 10:1–6 is summarized as a deliberate Christlikeness — living and serving in such a way as to seek God's approval, described as desiring "God's smile."

context: the meekness and gentleness of Christ
point_summary: Paul's appeal to the Corinthians is grounded explicitly in "the meekness and gentleness of Christ," presenting Christlikeness—not personal authority—as the governing model for his ministry approach.

context: for though we walk in the flesh, we do not war after the flesh
point_summary: Though believers live in the flesh, Paul draws a sharp distinction between physical existence and the manner of spiritual warfare, which operates on an entirely different plane than carnal methods.

context: for the weapons of our warfare are not carnal, but mighty through God to the pulling down of strongholds
point_summary: Spiritual warfare employs weapons that are not carnal but divinely powerful, targeting strongholds, imaginations, and every thought raised against the knowledge of God.

context: casting down imaginations and every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God, and bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ
point_summary: Spiritual warfare, Paul argues, operates not through carnal means but through God-empowered weapons that pull down strongholds and bring every thought captive to Christ's obedience.

context: but mighty, notice this, through God to the pulling down of strongholds
point_summary: Spiritual warfare is not won by forceful personality or fleshly power but by weapons that are mighty through God, capable of pulling down strongholds in individuals, families, and nations.

context: through God to the pulling down of strongholds
point_summary: Spiritual weapons, not carnal force or commanding personality, are what demolish strongholds — and such power operates only through God, not through human strength or impressive presence.

context: his strength is made perfect through weakness. When I am weak, then am I strong
point_summary: God's strength made perfect in weakness (2 Cor. 12:9) grounds the argument that spiritual warfare is won through dependence on God, not through impressive human persona or carnal force.

context: casting down imaginations, and notice he says, every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God, and bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ
point_summary: Spiritual warfare requires actively casting down every thought and imagination that exalts itself against God's knowledge, bringing the mind into captivity to Christ's obedience.

context: 2 Corinthians 10 in verse five, that we're to cast down imaginations, and we're to cast down every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God, and then we're to bring into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ
point_summary: Because spiritual warfare is fought in the mind, verse 5 calls believers to the active, disciplined work of demolishing every thought that rises against the knowledge of God and bringing every thought into submission to Christ.

context: verse number six and having in a readiness to revenge all disobedience when your obedience is fulfilled
point_summary: Paul's readiness to confront disobedience in verse 6 is framed as the natural outworking of a ministry goal—presenting every person mature in Christ and living in obedience pleasing to God.

context: having in a readiness to revenge all disobedience when your obedience is fulfilled
point_summary: Readiness to confront disobedience is inseparable from the goal of full obedience, since Paul's preaching against sin aims at bringing believers to spiritual maturity and completeness.

context: do you do ye look on things after the outward appearance
point_summary: Judging Paul's ministry by outward appearance alone missed the deeper reality of his God-given authority, which he grounds in his belonging to Christ equally with his critics.

context: do you do ye look on things after the outward appearance
point_summary: Judging by outward appearance alone is a human limitation, but Paul insists his standing in Christ is equal to that of any who boasted of their own credentials.

context: for though I should boast somewhat more of our authority which the Lord hath given us for edification and not for your destruction I should not be ashamed
point_summary: Apostolic authority is God-given and directed toward edification, not destruction — a foundation that makes bold, unashamed proclamation of God's Word both legitimate and necessary.

context: in verse number 8 again he says of our authority which the Lord hath given us
point_summary: Authority given by God carries a defined purpose — edification, building up rather than tearing down — and recognizing that purpose guards against the misuse of spiritual authority.

context: that I may not seem as if I would terrify you by letters

context: Look at what the Bible says in 2 Corinthians chapter four

context: in 2 Corinthians chapter number four, in verse number four, in whom the, you see it's a little g God, that's Satan, in whom the God of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them
point_summary: Satan's blinding work, identified in 2 Corinthians 4:4 as the agenda of the "little g" god of this world, is cited as the ultimate reason false teaching proliferates—keeping people from the gospel's light even through religious activity.

context: the little g God of this world wants you to be blind
point_summary: Satan's blindness strategy, per 2 Corinthians 4:4, targets not the irreligious but the religiously active — keeping people in dead orthodoxy and false gospels rather than personal faith in Christ.

context: lest the light of the glorious gospel should shine unto them
point_summary: Satan's blinding work is invoked through the phrase "lest the light of the glorious gospel should shine unto them" to explain why multitudes remain deceived by false teaching and dead religion.

context: though our outward man may perish, the inward man is to be renewed day by day, and we are to faint not
point_summary: Inward renewal day by day, drawn from 2 Corinthians 4:16, grounds the sermon's definition of true success as soul-prosperity rather than outward accumulation.

context: Notice over in 2 Corinthians chapter number 4, the Bible tells us as Paul is writing here
point_summary: Ministry is not self-appointed but received by mercy, and Paul's point in 2 Corinthians 4:1 is that this God-given stewardship is the reason ministers do not faint.

context: he says in 2 Corinthians 4 and verse number 1, therefore seeing, notice, we have this ministry. As we have received mercy, we faint not.
point_summary: Ministry of reconciliation belongs to the whole church, not just apostles or pastors, since Paul addresses believers at Corinth when he declares "we have this ministry."

context: if you look at chapter number 5, he says this in verse 18, he says, and all things are of God who hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ, notice this, and hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation.
point_summary: God entrusts the ministry of reconciliation not to apostles or church leaders alone, but to all believers, since Paul addresses this charge to the entire church at Corinth.

context: God has given us this ministry and that the ministry that he's given to us is the ministry of reconciliation
point_summary: The ministry of reconciliation, given by God to all believers in the church at Corinth—not merely to pastors or leaders—is identified as the defining content of "the ministry" Paul references in 2 Corinthians 4:1.

context: if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. Old things are passed away, behold, all things are become new
point_summary: Salvation is itself a great miracle — the new creation of 2 Corinthians 5:17 — and should move believers to tell everyone, just as the healing in Mark 9 would have.

context: they are to not to compare themselves amongst themselves
point_summary: Self-examination is not a matter of comparing oneself to others — which Paul calls foolish — but of measuring one's actions, words, and thoughts against whether they please God.

context: they have been blinded by the God of this world
point_summary: Satan's blinding work (2 Cor. 4:4) is invoked to explain why many in society embrace unbiblical views of marriage, calling for gracious truth-telling rather than ugliness toward the deceived.

context: God loveth a cheerful giver
point_summary: David's example in 1 Chronicles 29 is cited as the Bible's clearest portrait of the cheerful giver commended in 2 Corinthians 9:7, grounding financial generosity in the recognition that all things originate with God.

context: the Bible tells us that our God is the God of all comfort
point_summary: God is identified as "the God of all comfort" who comforts believers in all tribulation rather than removing it — a distinction the sermon treats as foundational to understanding divine provision.

context: he comforteth us in all our tribulation
point_summary: God's comfort in tribulation is distinguished from removal of tribulation — He comforts us *in* our trouble, not by eliminating it.

context: The Bible tells us in 2 Corinthians in chapter number six about the coming of Titus
point_summary: God's provision extends beyond material needs to include encouragement, illustrated by how He comforted Paul through the arrival of Titus in 2 Corinthians 6.

context: 2 Corinthians chapter 12 when Paul is speaking about the affliction that he's dealing with and this thorn in his side
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

point_summary: Believers have been explicitly commanded to go and tell—unlike the lepers who acted on reason alone—making silence inexcusable for those who know the gospel and have been sent as ambassadors.

context: let a man examine himself
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for to be absent from the body is

point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we looked at 1 Corinthians and 2 Corinthians
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We are laborers together with him, with God

context: we're laborers together with God
point_summary: Receiving God's grace in vain is implicitly warned against when believers fail to cooperate with one another and with God, since the church's mission demands partnership, not isolated effort.

context: reminded of what the truth is in 2 Corinthians 4 that the God of this world has blinded the minds of them that believe not
point_summary: When society removes the Creator and the account of sin's origin, 2 Corinthians 4:4 explains the result: Satan has blinded unbelievers' minds, creating a felt needlessness for a Redeemer.

context: We started looking at it in 2 Corinthians 8 a few weeks ago
point_summary: The collection Paul was carrying to Jerusalem represented a ministry obligation he had been entrusted with, and his request for prayer centered on that service being accepted by the saints.

context: If any man be in Christ, he's a new creature. Old things are passed away. Behold, all things are become new.
