context: great is my boldness of speech toward you. Great is my glorying of you. I am filled with comfort. I am exceeding joyful in all our tribulation
point_summary: Even amid tribulation, Paul declares himself "exceeding joyful," countering the assumption that joy requires the absence of suffering rather than coexisting with it.

context: when we were come into Macedonia, our flesh had no rest, but we were troubled. Notice what he says, on every side. Without were fightings, within were fears.
point_summary: Even in Paul's extremity — flesh with no rest, fightings without and fears within — God's comfort came not directly but through the arrival of a person, Titus, establishing that God uses people to encourage His servants.

context: God, who is the God of all comfort
point_summary: God is introduced in 2 Corinthians 1:3 as "the God of all comfort," a title the sermon connects to His provision of human encouragers like Titus to sustain Paul amid tribulation on every side.

context: I'm willing, as Paul said, to spend and be spent
point_summary: Willingness to "spend and be spent" (2 Cor. 12:15) is held up as the posture behind effective ministry, linking Paul's self-giving effort to Onesiphorus's diligent seeking and refreshing of Paul.

context: Paul testifies in 2 Corinthians that he was dealing with all of this opposition and all this difficulty, but the reality is that he did not faint
point_summary: Paul's testimony in 2 Corinthians that he endured opposition and difficulty without fainting is cited as evidence that spiritual strength from the Lord sustains believers through adversity.

context: I'm confident, willing, to be absent of the body, to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Death for the believer is not defeat but deliverance—a transition from bodily absence to the Lord's presence, which Paul calls "far better."

context: my grace is sufficient for you, for thee
point_summary: God's grace declared sufficient in 2 Corinthians 12:9 is cited as a foundational promise of comfort before the sermon moves into its primary focus on Jehovah Ra and Psalm 23.

context: for he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: Christ's sinless life and substitutionary death are presented as the necessary payment that makes the Savior of the world available as a personal Savior to each individual sinner.

context: He hath made Him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in Him
point_summary: Christ's substitutionary work in 2 Corinthians 5:21 is cited as the ground of the believer's imputed righteousness, which qualifies them as the "righteous" upon whom God's eyes rest in Psalm 34:15.

context: If any man be in Christ, he is a new creature, amen? Old things are passed away, behold all things become new.
point_summary: Regeneration in Christ is the only answer to human wickedness, because new life transforms sinners into new creatures—the sole hope for any nation.

context: 2 Corinthians five in verse number 15
point_summary: Christ's atoning death obligates believers to abandon self-directed living and devote themselves entirely to the One who died and rose for them.

context: and that he died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which died for them and rose again
point_summary: Christ's atoning death obligates believers to abandon self-directed living and devote themselves entirely to the One who died and rose for them.

context: verse 17. If any man be in Christ, he's a new creature
point_summary: Regeneration in Christ — becoming a new creature — grounds the believer's responsibility to live not for self but for the One who died and rose for them.

context: as being ambassadors for Christ in verse number 20
point_summary: Ambassadorship for Christ in 2 Corinthians 5:20 is cited as the believer's responsibility and position, grounding the call to live for God rather than self.

context: he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: The imputed righteousness of Christ — he was made sin so that believers might be made the righteousness of God — is presented as the motivating ground for living wholly unto God rather than oneself.

context: we were looking there at that first section of the 13th chapter as it wraps up, and Paul's talking about, I'm coming to you, this is the third time
point_summary: The immutability of God's counsel, drawn from 2 Corinthians 13 alongside Proverbs 19:21, underscores that Israel's failure was not ignorance of God's word but willful refusal to walk in it.

context: our theme all year from 2nd Corinthians, been preaching through the book of 2nd Corinthians on Sunday mornings
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: as we studied recently in 2 Corinthians 5, for he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: Christ's voluntary substitutionary death is grounded in 2 Corinthians 5:21, where the sinless Son was made sin so that believers might receive the righteousness of God in him.

context: to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Knowing Christ as the conquering Shepherd removes the fear of death, because to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord—death for the believer is gain, not loss.

context: every promise of God and him is yea

context: God the father made him God the son to be sin for us. Who knew no sin that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: At Calvary, God made Christ to be sin on behalf of sinners so that guilty souls might receive the righteousness of God — the great exchange that grounds repentance, surrender, and worship.

context: He made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: The wrath of God poured upon Christ at Calvary—he who knew no sin made sin for us—is presented as a suffering surpassing even the physical torment of the cross.

context: Turn over there in 2 Corinthians chapter number four in verse number 18
point_summary: Fixing one's gaze on unseen, eternal realities rather than visible, temporal accomplishments reflects the value system God uses, in contrast to the world's.

context: We find in 2 Corinthians four in verse number 17, he says, for our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory
point_summary: Temporal accomplishments hold no eternal weight, but present afflictions—however light and momentary—are working toward a far more exceeding and eternal glory awaiting the believer beyond death.

context: while we look not at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen. For the things which are seen are temporal. The things which are not seen are eternal
point_summary: Fixing one's focus on unseen, eternal realities rather than temporal ones is the perspective that distinguishes the godly line from the ungodly, and the one God calls His people to maintain today.

context: to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord
point_summary: Paul's confidence that physical death means immediate presence with the Lord grounds the sermon's call to run the Christian course faithfully, knowing departure brings certain reward.

context: the little g God of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not
point_summary: Satan's blinding of unbelieving minds (2 Cor. 4:4) is cited as the reason truth must be delivered with compassion, since those without it are spiritually darkened and in need of the profitable word.

context: he died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto him which loved them, and he died for us, and he rose again
point_summary: Christ's death for all obligates believers to live not for themselves but for him, providing the motivating foundation for finishing one's course well.

point_summary: Paul's call to biblical separation in 2 Corinthians 6 is often partially quoted, obscuring that it addresses both ecclesiastical and personal separation as distinct but related duties.

context: O ye Corinthians, our mouth is open unto you, our heart is enlarged
point_summary: Paul's open mouth and enlarged heart toward the Corinthians demonstrate his genuine pastoral care for them, while their constraint lies not in him but in themselves.

point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: as we read in chapter two and entering into chapter three of the book of second Corinthians
point_summary: Before defending his ministry, Paul examined his own heart first, then explained himself to the Corinthians—modeling self-examination as the prerequisite to restoring strained fellowship.

context: as we read in chapter two and entering into chapter three of the book of second Corinthians
point_summary: Before answering his accusers, Paul first turned inward in self-examination, a pattern the sermon traces from 2 Corinthians 2–3 as foundational to restoring strained fellowship.

context: he gives even further truth in chapter 12, hold your place here, but look at second Corinthians chapter 12
point_summary: Paul's deep anguish over the strained fellowship with the Corinthians—caused by those who opposed him—weighed heavily on his heart, as further evidenced in chapter 12.

context: the 12th chapter, notice with me in verse number 14, the Bible says, behold, the third time I am ready to come to you and I will not be burdensome to you
point_summary: Paul's parental love for the Corinthians—seeking not their possessions but their souls—drove his willingness to spend himself entirely for them, even when that love was met with diminished affection in return.

context: for I seek not yours, but you. For the children ought not to lay up for the parents, but the parents for the children. And I will very gladly spend and be spent
point_summary: Paul's willingness to spend and be spent for the Corinthians, even while receiving less love in return, illustrates the depth of pastoral care rooted in spiritual responsibility and accountability before Christ.

context: though the more abundantly I love you, the less I be loved
point_summary: Paul's willingness to spend and be spent for the Corinthians, even while receiving less love in return, illustrates his God-entrusted responsibility to shepherd them toward completeness before Christ.

context: our mouth is open unto you. He continues in verse 11 and says, our heart is enlarged
point_summary: Paul's open mouth and enlarged heart toward the Corinthians demonstrate that the relational barrier blocking their fellowship was on their side, not his, and he pleads for their hearts to be opened so they can receive what God has for them.

context: ye are not straightened in us. Again, it's not the fact that I have something against you, but ye are straightened in your own bowels, not for a recompense in the same. I speak as unto my children. Be ye also enlarged
point_summary: Paul's appeal in verse 12 locates the relational breakdown not in his own heart but in the Corinthians' own closed affections, as he pleads for restored fellowship so they can receive what God has for them.

context: My heart's, my mouth is open unto you. Our heart is enlarged
point_summary: Paul's open mouth and enlarged heart toward the Corinthians (v. 11) demonstrate that genuine care must precede counsel, establishing the relational foundation for his coming call to separation.

context: Be ye also enlarged
point_summary: Paul's appeal in verse 13 — "be ye also enlarged" — grounds the call to separation in mutual affection, establishing that his counsel flows from demonstrated care, not cold command.

context: be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers. For what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness and what communion hath light with darkness?
point_summary: Paul's command not to be unequally yoked with unbelievers is framed as pastoral counsel that carries weight precisely because it flows from demonstrated love and care for the Corinthians.

context: For ye are the temple of the living God and as God hath said, I will dwell in them and walk in them and I will be their God and they shall be mine. My people
point_summary: The identity of believers as the temple of the living God, in whom God dwells and walks, grounds the call to separation from unbelievers as a matter of covenant belonging.

context: wherefore, come out from among them, and be ye separate, saith the Lord, and touch not the unclean thing, and I will receive you and will be a father unto you
point_summary: The command to "come out and be separate" in verse 17 is presented as a restatement and reinforcement of the counsel already given in verse 14 against being unequally yoked.

context: and you shall be my sons and daughters, saith the Lord Almighty
point_summary: God's promise to be a Father and to receive believers as sons and daughters (v. 18) is presented as the motivating reward that undergirds the call to separation stated in verses 14 and 17.

context: having therefore these promises, dearly beloved. Let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God
point_summary: The promises of God's fatherly reception and indwelling serve as the motivating foundation for the command to cleanse oneself from all filthiness of flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God.

context: be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers
point_summary: Paul's command not to be unequally yoked with unbelievers serves as the foundational counsel of the passage, restated in verse 17 and then unpacked through a series of rhetorical questions that make the incompatibility self-evident.

context: wherefore, come out from among them, and be ye separate, saith the Lord, and touch not the unclean thing, and I will receive you
point_summary: Verse 17 restates the command of verse 14 in different words, showing that the call to separation from unbelievers is the sermon's central, repeated counsel to the Corinthians.

context: be not unequally yoked together with unbelievers
point_summary: The command "be not unequally yoked together with unbelievers" in verse 14 is the foundational counsel of the passage, which Paul then reinforces through a series of rhetorical questions designed not to gather information but to provoke the readers' own reflection on the incompatibility of righteousness and unrighteousness.

context: for what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness?
point_summary: The command "be not unequally yoked with unbelievers" in verse 14 is the core counsel of the passage, which Paul then supports through a series of rhetorical questions designed not to inform but to provoke the reader's own contemplation of the truth.

context: what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness? He's not really necessarily seeking information or expecting them to answer, but he's trying to communicate to them the truth that hey, why is the counsel to be separate? Why is the counsel to be not unequally yoked together with unbelievers?
point_summary: Paul's rhetorical question — "what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness?" — is presented not to gather information but to provoke the Corinthians to recognize the self-evident incompatibility underlying the command to separate.

context: what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness
point_summary: The rhetorical questions of verse 14 — "what fellowship hath righteousness with unrighteousness?" and "what communion hath light with darkness?" — are pressed as self-answering arguments that the incompatibility between believers and unbelievers grounds the command to separate.

context: What communion hath light with darkness?
point_summary: The rhetorical questions of verse 14 — what fellowship has righteousness with unrighteousness, and what communion has light with darkness — are used to establish that separation from unbelievers is grounded in an absolute, inherent incompatibility between the two.

context: what communion hath light with darkness
point_summary: The rhetorical questions of verse 14 — what fellowship has righteousness with unrighteousness, and what communion has light with darkness — establish that no common ground exists between the believer's new nature and the darkness they have left behind.

context: no fellowship between righteousness and unrighteousness. There's no communion between light and darkness
point_summary: The rhetorical questions of verse 14 — what fellowship has righteousness with unrighteousness, and what communion has light with darkness — establish that no common ground exists between the believer's new nature and the darkness they have left behind.

context: you're a new creature. Old things are passed away
point_summary: The regenerate believer's complete transformation in Christ — old things passed away, all things new — establishes why no true communion exists between the believer's present identity and the darkness of unbelief.

context: old things are passed away, and behold, all things are become new
point_summary: The regenerate believer's radical break from the old life — "what I was is not what I am" — grounds the call to separation, since the new creature in Christ has no basis for fellowship with unrighteousness.

context: verse 15 with an additional question
point_summary: The incompatibility of Christ and Belial illustrates that salvation produces a fundamental change of nature, making any agreement or union between the believer and the unbeliever impossible.

context: what concord hath Christ with Belial
point_summary: The incompatibility of Christ and Belial illustrates that salvation produces a fundamental change of nature, making any agreement or union between the believer and the unbeliever impossible.

context: what concord hath Christ with Belial? Well, that's pretty direct, is it not? I mean, very forward as Paul is providing this truth of being not unequally yoked together with unbelievers
point_summary: The command not to be unequally yoked with unbelievers is grounded in the fundamental difference between the saved and the unsaved — a difference that makes genuine spiritual fellowship or agreement impossible.

context: What part hath he that believeth with an infidel?
point_summary: Paul's two rhetorical questions in this verse — what agreement exists between Christ and Belial, and what part has a believer with an infidel — press the Corinthians to recognize that salvation creates a fundamental incompatibility between believers and unbelievers that rules out being yoked together with them.

context: What agreement hath the temple of God with idols?
point_summary: The rhetorical question "What agreement hath the temple of God with idols?" establishes that believers, as God's temple, have no basis for fellowship or agreement with idolatry in any form.

context: what agreement hath the temple of God with idols
point_summary: God's temple and idols are fundamentally incompatible, and just as Old Testament revivals required cleansing idols from the temple, believers must continually guard against worldly things creeping back into their lives.

context: what agreement hath the temple of God with idols?
point_summary: Because believers are themselves the temple of the living God, introducing idols into that temple is as spiritually catastrophic as the literal defilement of Israel's temple that required repeated cleansing.

context: ye are the temple of the living God
point_summary: Paul's declaration that believers are "the temple of the living God" carries the weight of Old Testament temple history, making the intrusion of idols into that living temple as grievous as the literal idols that defiled Solomon's temple.

context: 2 Corinthians chapter 6, as he's asked these questions and he says, what agreement, in verse 16, hath the temple of God with idols? And then he says, for ye are the temple of the living God
point_summary: Because believers are the temple of the living God—in whom He dwells and walks—any agreement between that temple and idols is incompatible with their identity as His people.

context: as God hath said, I will dwell in them and walk in them and I will be their God and they shall be my people
point_summary: Because believers are the temple of the living God in whom He dwells and walks, any agreement between that temple and idols is incompatible with their identity and calling to separation.

context: wherefore, come out from among them and be ye separate, saith the Lord, and touch not the unclean thing and I will receive you
point_summary: The command to "come out and be separate" in verse 17 is addressed to already-saved believers whose fellowship with God is conditioned on separation from worldly influence, with the accompanying promises of divine reception and fatherly relationship serving as the motivation.

context: and will be a father unto you. You shall be my sons and daughters, saith the Lord Almighty
point_summary: God's promise to be a Father and to receive His people as sons and daughters is the relational reward held out as motivation for the separation commanded in the preceding verse.

context: having therefore these promises, dearly beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God
point_summary: Because believers already possess the promises of God's fatherly fellowship and reception, Paul calls them to actively cleanse themselves from all defilement of flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God.

context: come out from among them and be separate
point_summary: The command to "come out and be separate" in verse 17 is presented not as a call to salvation but as a call to restored fellowship, with God's promised reception and fatherhood as the motivating reward for obedience.

context: I will receive you, that I will be a father unto you, you shall be my sons and daughters, saith the Lord Almighty
point_summary: The promises of God's fatherhood and fellowship in verse 18 are presented not as salvation promises but as incentives to separation—the communion believers forfeit when sin hinders their walk with a holy God.

context: having therefore these promises, dearly beloved, let us cleanse ourselves
point_summary: The promises of God's covenant fellowship motivate believers to pursue ongoing cleansing from defilement—not for salvation, but for the sanctification that restores intimate communion with a holy God.

context: come out from among them, be not unequally yoked
point_summary: Separation from ungodly associations is grounded in the believer's new identity in Christ, which creates an irreconcilable incompatibility between what the saved person now is and what they once were.

context: don't be tainted by the world. Don't allow the world to influence the church
point_summary: The command not to be unequally yoked with unbelievers is grounded in the irreconcilable incompatibility between belief and unbelief, leading to the practical conclusion that separation is inevitable—if not chosen, then imposed by the world itself.

point_summary: Fixing one's gaze on eternal rather than temporal realities—as 2 Corinthians 4:18 instructs—transforms perspective during tribulation, since heaven is forever while present suffering is not.

context: For he hath made him to be sin for us who knew no sin, that we might be made the righteousness of God in him
point_summary: Imputed righteousness through Christ's atoning work is the positional standing every believer receives at salvation, distinct from the experiential rewards or losses assessed at the judgment seat of Christ.

context: We'll deal with that more as we continue in 2 Corinthians 8
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: therefore, if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature. Old things are passed away, behold, all things will become new
point_summary: Being "in Christ," like being in the ark, is a supernatural transformation — not self-improvement or therapy — in which God makes a person an entirely new creature.

context: Second Corinthians chapter number seven. And be looking at verses two through 16 this morning as we finish this passage of scripture.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We entered into the first verse last Sunday morning as we really focused at the end of chapter six and entered into the first verse of chapter seven
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: entered into the first verse of chapter seven and looked at that truth of biblical separation
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the Apostle Paul picks up here where he left off in chapter two
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: in chapter one and verse number 12 all the way through chapter two and verse number 13
point_summary: Paul's defense of his conduct in 2 Corinthians 1:12–2:13 is identified as the textual block he set aside and now resumes addressing in chapter seven.

context: in verse 11 of chapter six how his mouth was open unto them, how his heart was enlarged
point_summary: Paul's open mouth and enlarged heart toward the Corinthians (6:11) illustrate his deep pastoral desire to impart spiritual truth, even amid the relational strain that made some unwilling to receive his message.

context: receive us. We have wronged no man. We have corrupted no man. We have defrauded no man.
point_summary: Paul's opening plea — "receive us" — frames the entire passage as a call to restore strained relationships rather than resign to them with an "it is what it is" attitude.

context: I speak not this to condemn you. For I have said before that you are in our hearts to die and live with you.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I am exceeding joyful in all our tribulation. For when we were come into Macedonia, our flesh had no rest, but we were troubled on every side. Without were fightings. Within were fears.
point_summary: Even amid tribulation, Paul's joy overflows because his heart remains open toward the Corinthians, modeling that genuine boldness and comfort coexist with a deep desire for relational restoration.

context: Nevertheless, God that comforteth those that are cast down, comforted us by the coming of Titus.
point_summary: God's comfort reaches even the most distressed servant through human presence, as Paul identifies Titus's arrival in Macedonia as the specific means by which God comforted those who are cast down.

context: Notice in verse eight, for though I made you sorry with a letter, I do not repent, though I did repent, for I perceive that the same epistle hath made you sorry, though it were, but I do not repent
point_summary: Delivering hard truth to someone you love can produce internal conflict—a momentary regret—even when that truth is Spirit-inspired and necessary for the relationship's restoration.

context: Verse nine, now I rejoice. Not that you were made sorry, but notice when he says, but that you sorrowed to repentance
point_summary: Godly sorrow's value lies not in the pain it causes but in the repentance it produces, which is why Paul rejoices over the Corinthians' response to his hard letter.

context: I'm rejoicing because the sorrow work towards repentance for you were made sorry after a godly manner
point_summary: Genuine sorrow over sin is distinguished from mere regret at being caught, because only godly sorrow—not worldly sorrow—produces repentance that restores right standing before God.

context: Verse 10, for godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation not to be repented of, but the sorrow of the world worketh death
point_summary: Godly sorrow differs from worldly sorrow in that it produces genuine repentance—not mere regret at being caught—and operates in two spheres: initial salvation and ongoing fellowship with God.

context: godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation not to be repented of
point_summary: Godly sorrow differs from worldly sorrow in that it produces repentance leading to restored fellowship and joy, while worldly sorrow leads to death because the unbeliever has no knowledge of God's forgiveness.

context: Verse 11, for behold this self same thing that you sorrowed after a godly sort with carefulness it wrought in you, yea, what clearing of yourselves, yea, what indignation, yea, what fear, yea, what vehement desire, yea, what zeal, yea, what revenge, in all things you have approved yourselves to be clear in this matter.
point_summary: Godly sorrow producing true repentance yields a cluster of visible fruits—carefulness, indignation, fear, zeal—that together demonstrate restored fellowship and a cleared conscience before God.

context: Notice what he says at the end of verse 12, but for our care for you in the sight of God might appear unto you.
point_summary: Paul's stated motive for writing the painful letter was not to adjudicate blame between the offender and the offended, but to make his genuine care for the Corinthians visible before God.

context: therefore, we are comforted in your comfort, yea, and exceedingly the more joyed we for the joy of Titus because his spirit was refreshed by you all
point_summary: The reconciliation of the Corinthian church refreshed Titus's spirit, and that comfort then flowed back to Paul, illustrating how restored relationships produce a chain of mutual encouragement.

context: his inward affection is more abundant toward you whilst he remembereth the obedience of you all, how with fear and trembling ye received him
point_summary: Titus's deepened affection for the Corinthians, stirred by their fearful and trembling reception of him, became evidence to Paul that his prior boasting about them was vindicated and his confidence in them well-placed.

context: and I wrote this same unto you, lest when I came, I should have sorrow from them of whom I ought to rejoice, having confidence in you all, that my joy is the joy of you all
point_summary: Paul's confidence in the Corinthians—that their joy and his joy are bound together—is cited as a parallel to his closing declaration of confidence in chapter seven, showing the relational thread running through the entire letter.

context: in 2 Corinthians chapter number six and seven, excuse me, in verse number two, receive us
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: for I have said before that ye are in our hearts to die and live with you
context: He says, for I have said before that ye are in our hearts to die and live with you.
point_summary: Paul's declaration in verse 3 — that the Corinthians are in his heart to die and live with — is presented as evidence of a deep, self-denying craving for restored relationship rather than a willingness to simply move on.

reference_text: therefore we are comforted in your comfort, yea, and exceedingly the more joyed we for the joy of Titus, because his spirit was refreshed by you all
context: Paul says in verse 13 of this text of Scripture, therefore we are comforted in your comfort, yea, and exceedingly the more joyed we for the joy of Titus, because his spirit was refreshed by you all.
point_summary: Paul's comfort in verse 13 is presented as evidence of his craving for restored relationship, showing that reconciliation with the Corinthians brought him joy through Titus's refreshed spirit.

context: chapter number seven, the text in which we're looking at this morning
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: therefore seeing we have this ministry, as we have received mercy, we faint not
point_summary: Ministry entrusted through mercy—not personal merit—is the stated basis for why Paul and his co-workers do not lose heart in their apostolic calling.

context: and all things are of God who hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ and hath given to us the ministry of reconciliation
point_summary: The ministry of reconciliation in 2 Corinthians 5:18 refers specifically to God reconciling sinners to Himself through Christ, not to restoring horizontal relationships between believers.

context: ambassadors
point_summary: Believers commissioned as ambassadors of reconciliation between God and sinners (5:20) are thereby disqualified from that ministry when unresolved conflict with a brother remains unaddressed.

context: God said his grace is sufficient
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.
